entries.english: The dervish, then, is he that has no intermediary: the flames have connection with his being.

entries.farsi: پس دل عالم وى است ايرا كه تن / مى‏رسد از واسطه‏ى اين دل به فن
entries.english: Therefore he is the heart of the world, because by means of this heart the body attains to art.

entries.farsi: دل نباشد، تن چه داند گفت‏وگو / دل نجويد، تن چه داند جستجو
entries.english: The heart is not there, how can the body talk and speak? The heart seeks not, how can the body seek and search?

entries.farsi: پس نظرگاه شعاع آن آهن است / ‏پس نظرگاه خدا دل نى تن است
entries.english: Therefore the theatre of the rays is that iron; therefore the theatre of God is the heart, not the body.

entries.farsi: باز اين دلهاى جزوى چون تن است / ‏با دل صاحب دلى كاو معدن است
entries.english: Again, these partial hearts are as the body in relation to the heart of the man of heart, which is the original source

entries.farsi: بس مثال و شرح خواهد اين كالم / ‏ليك ترسم تا نلغزد وهم عام
entries.english: This argument wants much illustration and exposition, but I fear lest the opinion of the vulgar should stumble;

entries.farsi: تا نگردد نيكويى ما بدى / اينكه گفتم هم نبد جز بى‏خودى
entries.english: Unless my goodness should be turned to badness; even this that I have spoken was nothing but selflessness.

entries.farsi: پاى كج را كفش كج بهتر بود / مر گدا را دستگه بر در بود
entries.english: The crooked shoe is better for the crooked foot; the beggar’s power reaches only as far as the door.

sections.title_fa: امتحان پادشاه به آن دو غالم كه نو خريده بود
sections.title_en: How the King made trial of the two slaves whom he had recently purchased

entries.farsi: پادشاهى دو غالم ارزان خريد / با يكى ز آن دو سخن گفت و شنيد
entries.english: A King bought two slaves cheap, and conversed with one of the two.

entries.farsi: آدمى مخفى است در زير زبان / ‏اين زبان پرده است بر درگاه جان
entries.english: He found him quick-witted and answering sweetly: what issues from the sugar-lip? Sugar-water

entries.farsi: يافتش زيرك دل و شيرين جواب / ‏از لب شكر چه زايد شكر آب
entries.english: Man is concealed underneath his tongue: this tongue is the curtain over the gate of the soul.

entries.farsi: كاندر آن خانه گهر يا گندم است / ‏گنج زر يا جمله مار و كژدم است
entries.english: When a gust of wind has rolled up the curtain, the secret of the interior of the house is disclosed to us,

entries.farsi: چون كه بادى پرده را در هم كشيد / سر صحن خانه شد بر ما پديد
entries.english: Whether in that house there are pearls or wheat, a treasure of gold or whether all is snakes and scorpions;

entries.farsi: يا در او گنج است و مارى بر كران / ‏ز انكه نبود گنج زر بى‏پاسبان
entries.english: Or whether a treasure is there and a serpent beside it, since a treasure of gold is not without some one to keep watch.

entries.farsi: بى‏تامل او سخن گفتى چنان / ‏كز پس پانصد تامل ديگران
entries.english: Without premeditation he would speak in such wise as others after five hundred premeditations.

entries.farsi: نور هر گوهر كز او تابان شدى / حق و باطل را از او فرقان شدى
entries.english: You would have said that in his inward part there was a sea, and that the whole sea was pearls of eloquence,

entries.farsi: گفتى اندر باطنش درياستى / ‏جمله دريا گوهر گوياستى
entries.english: The light that shone from every pearl became a criterion for distinguishing between truth and falsehood.

entries.farsi: نور فرقان فرق كردى بهر ما / ذره ذره حق و باطل را جدا
entries.english: Would the light of the Criterion, distinguish for us truth and falsehood and separate them mote by mote;

entries.farsi: نور گوهر نور چشم ما شدى / ‏هم سؤال و هم جواب از ما بدى
entries.english: The light of the Pearl would become the light of our eyes both the question and the answer would be from us.

entries.farsi: چشم كژ كردى دو ديدى قرص ماه / ‏چون سؤال است اين نظر در اشتباه
entries.english: You have made your eyes awry and seen the moon’s disk double: this gazing in perplexity is like the question.

entries.farsi: راست گردان چشم را در ماهتاب / ‏تا يكى بينى تو مه را نك جواب
entries.english: Make your eyes straight in the moonshine; so that you may see the moon as one. Lo, the answer.

entries.farsi: فكرتت كه كژ مبين نيكو نگر / هست آن فكرت شعاع آن گهر
entries.english: Tell your thought not to see awry and to look well: that thought is the radiance of that Pearl.

entries.farsi: هر جوابى كان ز گوش آيد به دل / ‏چشم گفت از من شنو آن را بهل
entries.english: Whenever an answer comes to the heart through the ear, the eye says, “Hear it from me; let that alone!”

entries.farsi: گوش دالله ست و چشم اهل وصال / ‏چشم صاحب حال و گوش اصحاب قال
entries.english: The ear is a go-between, while the eye is possessed of union The eye has direct experience; while the ear has words.

entries.farsi: در شنود گوش تبديل صفات / ‏در عيان ديدها تبديل ذات
entries.english: In the ear’s hearing there is a transformation of qualities; in the eyes’ seeing there is a transformation of essence.

entries.farsi: ز آتش ار علمت يقين شد از سخن / ‏پختگى جو در يقين منزل مكن
entries.english: If your knowledge of fire has been turned to certainty by words, seek to be cooked, and do not abide in the certainty.

entries.farsi: تا نسوزى نيست آن عين اليقين / ‏اين يقين خواهى در آتش در نشين
entries.english: There is no intuitive certainty until you burn; you desire this certainty, sit down in the fire.

entries.farsi: گوش چون نافذ بود ديده شود / ور نه قل در گوش پيچيده شود
entries.english: When the ear is penetrating, it becomes an eye; otherwise, the word becomes entangled in the ear.

entries.farsi: اين سخن پايان ندارد باز گرد / تا كه شه با آن غالمانش چه كرد
entries.english: This discourse hath no end. Turn back, that what the King did to those slaves of his.

sections.title_fa: ‏به راه كردن شاه يكى را از آن دو غالم و از اين ديگر پرسيدن
sections.title_en: How the King sent away one of the two slaves and interrogated the other.

entries.farsi: آن غالمك را چو ديد اهل ذكا / آن دگر را كرد اشارت كه بيا
entries.english: When he saw that that young man was possessed of keen intelligence, he made a sign to the other to come.

entries.farsi: كاف رحمت گفتمش تصغير نيست / ‏جد چو گويد طفلكم تحقير نيست
entries.english: I have called him by the suffix of pity; it is not to belittle him: if a grandfather says my young friend, it is not contempt.

entries.farsi: چون بيامد آن دوم در پيش شاه / ‏بود او گنده دهان دندان سياه
entries.english: When the second came before the King, he had a stinking mouth and black teeth.

entries.farsi: گر چه شه ناخوش شد از گفتار او / جستجويى كرد هم ز اسرار او
entries.english: Although the King was displeased by his speech, still he made some inquiry concerning his hidden thoughts.

entries.farsi: گفت با اين شكل و اين گند دهان / ‏دور بنشين ليك آن سو تر مران
entries.english: He said, “With this aspect and this stinking mouth, sit at a distance, but do not move too far off—

entries.farsi: كه تو اهل نامه و رقعه بدى / ‏نه جليس و يار و هم بقعه بدى
entries.english: For you have been a writer of letters and notes; you have not been a companion and friend and comrade

entries.farsi: تا عالج آن دهان تو كنيم / ‏تو حبيب و ما طبيب پر فنيم
entries.english: That we may treat that mouth of yours: you are the beloved, and we are the skilful physician.

entries.farsi: بهر كيكى نو گليمى سوختن / نيست اليق از تو ديده دوختن
entries.english: It is not fitting to burn a new blanket on account of one flea; to shut my eyes to you.

entries.farsi: آن ذكى را پس فرستاد او به كار / سوى حمامى كه رو خود را بخار
entries.english: Notwithstanding all, sit down and talk on a few topics, that I may well see the form of your mind.”

entries.farsi: با همه بنشين دو سه دستان بگو / تا ببينم صورت عقلت نكو
entries.english: Then he sent that keen-witted one away to do: to a bath-house, saying, “Go, scrub yourself.”

entries.farsi: وين دگر را گفت خه تو زيركى / ‏صد غالمى در حقيقت نه يكى
entries.english: And to this other one he said, “Good! You are a clever lad: in truth you are a hundred slaves, not one.

entries.farsi: آن نه‏اى كه خواجه‏تاش تو نمود / از تو ما را سرد مى‏كرد آن حسود
entries.english: You are not such as your fellow-servant declared: that envious one would have me cold to you,

entries.farsi: گفت او دزد و كژ است و كژنشين / ‏حيز و نامرد و چنان است و چنين
entries.english: He said that you are thievish and dishonest and ill-behaved, immoral and infamous and so forth.”

entries.farsi: گفت پيوسته بده ست او راست گو / راست گويى من نديده ستم چو او
entries.english: The slave said, “He has always been veracious; I have not seen any one so truthful as he is.

entries.farsi: راست گويى در نهادش خلقتى است / ‏هر چه گويد من نگويم تهمتى است
entries.english: Veracity is inborn in his nature; whatever he says, I do not say it is void.

entries.farsi: كژ ندانم آن نكو انديش را / متهم دارم وجود خويش را
entries.english: I deem not that good-minded one malicious: I suspect my own person.

entries.farsi: باشد او در من ببيند عيبها / من نبينم در وجود خود شها
entries.english: Maybe, he sees in me faults I do not see in myself, O King.”

entries.farsi: هر كسى گر عيب خود ديدى ز پيش / ‏كى بدى فارغ خود از اصالح خويش
entries.english: Any one who saw his own faults before— how indeed should he be unconcerned with correcting himself?

entries.farsi: غافلند اين خلق از خود اى پدر / الجرم گويند عيب همدگر
entries.english: These people take no heed of themselves, O father: consequently they blame one another.

entries.farsi: من نبينم روى خود را اى شمن / ‏من ببينم روى تو تو روى من
entries.english: O idolater, I do not behold my own face, I behold your face and you behold mine.

entries.farsi: آن كسى كه او ببيند روى خويش / ‏نور او از نور خلقان است بيش
entries.english: He that beholds his own face — his light is greater than the light of the creatures.

entries.farsi: گر بميرد ديد او باقى بود / ز انكه ديدش ديد خالقى بود
entries.english: Though he dies, his sight is everlasting, because his sight is the sight of the Creator

entries.farsi: نور حسى نبود آن نورى كه او / روى خود محسوس بيند پيش رو
entries.english: That light, by which he sensibly beholds his own face before him, is not the light of sense.

entries.farsi: گفت اكنون عيبهاى او بگو / آن چنان كه گفت او از عيب تو
entries.english: The King said, “Now tell his faults, just as he spoke of yours,

entries.farsi: تا بدانم كه تو غم خوار منى / ‏كدخداى ملكت و كار منى
entries.english: That I may know whether you are solicitous for me and a house-steward of my property and business.”

entries.farsi: گفت اى شه من بگويم عيبهاش / ‏گر چه هست او مر مرا خوش خواجه‏تاش
entries.english: He replied, “O King, I will tell his faults, though he is to me a pleasing fellow-servant.

entries.farsi: عيب او مهر و وفا و مردمى / ‏عيب او صدق و ذكا و هم دمى
entries.english: His faults are affection and loyalty and humanity; his faults are sincerity and keen-wittedness and cordial comradeship.

entries.farsi: كمترين عيبش جوانمردى و داد / آن جوانمردى كه جان را هم بداد
entries.english: His least fault is generosity and bounty— the generosity that even gives up life.”

entries.farsi: صد هزاران جان خدا كرده پديد / چه جوانمردى بود كان را نديد
entries.english: God has brought to view myriads of lives; what generosity would there be that saw not those?

entries.farsi: ور بديدى كى به جان بخلش بدى / ‏بهر يك جان كى چنين غمگين شدى
entries.english: And if he saw them, how should he grudge his life? How should he become so grieved for the sake of one life?

entries.farsi: بر لب جو بخل آب آن را بود / كاو ز جوى آب نابينا بود
entries.english: On the river-bank, water is grudged by him that is blind to the stream of water.

entries.farsi: گفت پيغمبر كه هر كه از يقين / ‏داند او پاداش خود در يوم دين
entries.english: The Prophet said,” Whosoever knows for sure his recompense on the day of Resurrection

entries.farsi: كه يكى را ده عوض مى‏آيدش / ‏هر زمان جودى دگرگون زايدش
entries.english: That his compensation will be ten for one— at every moment a different munificence will issue from him.”

entries.farsi: جود جمله از عوضها ديدن است / ‏پس عوض ديدن ضد ترسيدن است
entries.english: All munificence is from seeing compensations; therefore seeing the compensation is opposed to fear.

entries.farsi: بخل ناديدن بود اعواض را / شاد دارد ديد در خواض را
entries.english: Miserliness consists in not seeing compensations: the prospect of pearls keeps the diver glad.

entries.farsi: پس به عالم هيچ كس نبود بخيل / ‏ز انكه كس چيزى نبازد بى‏بديل
entries.english: Hence no one in the world is miserly, since no one hazards anything without what is to be received in exchange.

entries.farsi: پس سخا از چشم آمد نه ز دست / ‏ديد دارد كار جز بينا نرست
entries.english: Therefore, generosity comes from the eye, not from the hand: it is seeing that matters; none but the seer is saved.

entries.farsi: عيب ديگر اين كه خود بين نيست او / هست او در هستى خود عيب جو
entries.english: “Another fault that he is not self-conceited; he is anxious to find fault with his self-existence.

entries.farsi: عيب گوى و عيب جوى خود بده ست / ‏با همه نيكو و با خود بد بده ست
entries.english: He has been one who speaks in blame of himself and seeks to blame himself; he has been good to all and bad to himself.”

entries.farsi: گفت شه جلدى مكن در مدح يار / مدح خود در ضمن مدح او ميار
entries.english: The King said, “Do not show eagerness in praising your friend, and do not introduce praise of yourself in the mask of praise of him;

entries.farsi: ز انكه من در امتحان آرم و را / شرمسارى آيدت در ما ورا
entries.english: Because I will bring him to the test and shame will befall you in the final result.”

sections.title_fa: قسم غالم در صدق و وفاى يار خود از طهارت ظن خود
sections.title_en: How the slave, from the purity of his thought, swore to the truth and loyalty of his friend.

entries.farsi: گفت نه و اهلل و باهلل العظيم / ‏مالِكَ الْمُلْكِ و به رحمان و رحيم
entries.english: He said, “No, by Allah, and by the great God, the possessor of kingdom, and by the Merciful and Compassionate One;

entries.farsi: آن خدايى كه فرستاد انبيا / نه به حاجت بل به فضل و كبريا
entries.english: By the God who sent the prophets, not in need, but in grace and majesty;

entries.farsi: آن خداوندى كه از خاك ذليل / ‏آفريد او شهسواران جليل
entries.english: By the Lord who from the lowly earth created glorious princely riders,

entries.farsi: پاكشان كرد از مزاج خاكيان / ‏بگذرانيد از تك افلاکيان
entries.english: Purified them from the temperament of earthly beings, and caused them to outrun the celestials;

entries.farsi: بر گرفت از نار و نور صاف ساخت / ‏وانگه او بر جمله‏ى انوار تاخت
entries.english: Who took up from the Fire and fashioned into pure Light— and then it outstripped all lights

entries.farsi: آن سنا برقى كه بر ارواح تافت / ‏تا كه آدم معرفت ز آن نور يافت
entries.english: That splendour of lightning which shone over the spirits, so that Adam gained from that Light knowledge.

entries.farsi: آن كز آدم رست و دست شيث چيد / پس خليفه‏ش كرد آدم كان بديد
entries.english: The hand of Seth gathered that which grew from Adam: therefore Adam, when he saw that, made him his vicar.

entries.farsi: جان ابراهيم از آن انوار زفت / ‏بى‏حذر در شعله‏هاى نار رفت
entries.english: Since Noah had enjoyment of that Jewel, he showered pearls in the air of the Sea of Soul.

entries.farsi: نوح از آن گوهر كه برخوردار بود / در هواى بحر جان دربار بود
entries.english: From that mighty radiance the spirit of Abraham went fearlessly into the flames of the fire.

entries.farsi: چون كه اسماعيل در جويش فتاد / پيش دشنه‏ى آب دارش سر نهاد
entries.english: When Ismail fell into the stream thereof, he laid his head before his flashing knife.

entries.farsi: جان داود از شعاعش گرم شد / آهن اندر دست بافش نرم شد
entries.english: The soul of David was heated by its rays: iron became soft in his hand-loom.

entries.farsi: چون سليمان بد وصالش را رضيع / ‏ديو گشتش بنده فرمان و مطيع
entries.english: When Solomon was suckled on union with it, the demon became a thrall to his command and obedient.

entries.farsi: در قضا يعقوب چون بنهاد سر / چشم روشن كرد از بوى پسر
entries.english: When Jacob bowed his head to the destiny, it illumined his eye with the scent of his son.

entries.farsi: يوسف مه رو چو ديد آن آفتاب / ‏شد چنان بيدار در تعبير خواب
entries.english: When the moon-faced Joseph beheld that Sun, he became so wide-awake in the interpretation of dreams.

entries.farsi: چون عصا از دست موسى آب خورد / ملكت فرعون را يك لقمه كرد
entries.english: When the rod drank water from the hand of Moses, it made one mouthful of Pharaoh’s empire.

entries.farsi: نردبانش عيسى مريم چو يافت / ‏بر فراز گنبد چارم شتافت
entries.english: When Jesus, the son of Mary, found its ladder, he sped to the topmost height of the Fourth Dome.

entries.farsi: چون محمد يافت آن ملك و نعيم / ‏قرص مه را كرد او در دم دو نيم
entries.english: When Mohammed gained that Kingdom and Felicity, he in a moment split the disk of the moon in two halves.

entries.farsi: حق و باطل را چو دل فاروق شد / ‏نور فايض بود و ذى النورين گشت
entries.english: When Abu Bakr became a signal example of favour, he became the Companion of such a King and Siddiq.

entries.farsi: چون ابو بكر آيت توفيق شد / با چنان شه صاحب و صديق شد
entries.english: When ‘Umar became distraught with that Beloved, he became a Faruq, like the heart, between truth and falsehood

entries.farsi: چون عمر شيداى آن معشوق شد / حق و باطل را چو دل فاروق شد
entries.english: When Uthman became the fountain of that clear, he was light overflowing and became Dhu ‘l-Nurayn.

entries.farsi: چون ز رويش مرتضى شد در فشان / ‏گشت او شير خدا درمرج جان
entries.english: When at its countenance Murtaza began to scatter pearls, he became the Lion of God in the pasture of the soul.

entries.farsi: چون جنيد از جند او ديد آن مدد / خود مقاماتش فزون شد از عدد
entries.english: When Junayd received that support from its army, his maqamat became innumerable.

entries.farsi: بايزيد اندر مزيدش راه ديد / نام قطب العارفين از حق شنيد
entries.english: Bayazid saw the way into its superabundance, and heard from God the name, “Pole of ’ the Gnostics.”

entries.farsi: چون كه كرخى كرخ او را شد حرص / ‏شد خليفه‏ى عشق و ربانى نفس
entries.english: When Karkhi became the guard of its city, he became the vicar of love and inspired by the Divine breath.

entries.farsi: و آن شقيق از شق آن راه شگرف / ‏گشت او خورشيد راى و تيز طرف
entries.english: The son of Adham joyously rode his steed towards it, and became the supreme sovereign of justice.

entries.farsi: پور ادهم مركب آن سو راند شاد / گشت او سلطان سلطانان داد
entries.english: And that Shaqiq by cleaving that venerable Way became a sun of judgment and keen-sighted

entries.farsi: صد هزاران پادشاهان نهان / ‏سر فرازانند ز آن سوى جهان
entries.english: Hundreds of thousands of hidden kings are holding their heads high beyond this world;

entries.farsi: نامشان از رشك حق پنهان بماند / هر گدايى نامشان را بر نخواند
entries.english: Because of God’s admiration their names remained hidden: every beggar did not pronounce their names.

entries.farsi: حق آن نور و حق نورانيان / ‏كاندر آن بحرند همچون ماهيان
entries.english: By the truth of that Light and by the truth of those illumined ones who are as fishes in that Sea

entries.farsi: بحر جان و جان بحر ار گويمش / ‏نيست اليق نام نو مى‏جويمش
entries.english: It is not fitting if I call it the Sea of the Soul and the Soul of the Sea: I am seeking a new name for it;—

entries.farsi: حق آن آنى كه اين و آن از اوست / ‏مغزها نسبت بدو باشد چو پوست
entries.english: By the truth of that that from which this and that are and in relation to which kernels are as rinds,

entries.farsi: كه صفات خواجه‏تاش و يار من / ‏هست صد چندان كه اين گفتار من
entries.english: That the qualities of my fellow-servant and friend exceed my description a hundredfold

entries.farsi: آن چه مى‏دانم ز وصف آن نديم / ‏باورت نايد چه گويم اى كريم
entries.english: That which I know of the endowments of that comrade would not be believed by thee: what shall I say, O noble?”

entries.farsi: شاه گفت اكنون از آن خود بگو / چند گويى آن اين و آن او
entries.english: The King said, “Now speak of your own; how long will you speak of what concerns this or that one?

entries.farsi: تو چه دارى و چه حاصل كرده‏اى / ‏از تگ دريا چه در آورده‏اى
entries.english: What do you possess and what have you gained? What pearls have you fetched from the bottom of the Sea?

entries.farsi: روز مرگ اين حس تو باطل شود / نور جان دارى كه يار دل شود
entries.english: On the day of death this sense-perception of yours will vanish: have you the spiritual light that should be the companion of your heart?

entries.farsi: در لحد كاين چشم را خاك آگند / هستت آن چه گور را روشن كند
entries.english: When dust shall fill these eyes in the tomb, have you that which will make the grave bright?

entries.farsi: آن زمان كه دست و پايت بر درد / پر و بالت هست تا جان بر پرد
entries.english: At the time when your hands and feet shall be torn in shreds, have you wings and feathers that your spirit may fly aloft?

entries.farsi: آن زمان كاين جان حيوانى نماند / جان باقى بايدت بر جا نشاند
entries.english: At the time when this animal soul is no more, it benefits you to set the everlasting spirit in its place

entries.farsi: شرط من جا بالحسن نه كردن است / ‏اين حسن را سوى حضرت بردن است
entries.english: The stipulation of he that comes with good does not consist in doing; it consists in bringing this good to the presence of God.

entries.farsi: جوهرى دارى ز انسان يا خرى / ‏اين عرضها كه فنا شد چون برى
entries.english: You have a substance human or asinine: how can you bring these accidents which have passed away?

entries.farsi: اين عرضهاى نماز و روزه را / ‏چون كه ال يبقى زمانين انتفى
entries.english: As regards these effects of prayer and fasting— since does not endure for two moments becomes naught

entries.farsi: تا مبدل گشت جوهر زين عرض / ‏چون ز پرهيزى كه زايل شد مرض
entries.english: It is impossible to carry over the effects; but they take away diseases from the substance,

entries.farsi: نقل نتوان كرد مر اعراض را / ليك از جوهر برند امراض را
entries.english: So that the essence becomes changed by means of this form, as when disease is removed by abstinence

entries.farsi: گشت پرهيز عرض جوهر به جهد / شد دهان تلخ از پرهيز شهد
entries.english: By exertion abstinence, the form becomes the essence: through abstinence the acrid mouth becomes honey.

entries.farsi: آن نكاح زن عرض بد شد فنا / جوهر فرزند حاصل شد ز ما
entries.english: Land, through sowing, is turned into ears of corn; remedies for the hair turn the hair into chains.

entries.farsi: از زراعت خاكها شد سنبله / ‏داروى مو كرد مو را سلسله
entries.english: Conjugal intercourse was the form; it passed away, and the result, the child, was produced from us.

entries.farsi: جفت كردن اسب و اشتر را عرض / ‏جوهر كره بزاييدن غرض
entries.english: The mating of horse or camel is the cause; the object is the birth of the colt, the result.

entries.farsi: هست آن بستان نشاندن هم عرض / گشت جوهر كشت بستان نك غرض
entries.english: Similarly, the planting of the garden is the cause; produce of the garden became the substance—behold the object!

entries.farsi: هم عرض دان كيميا بردن بكار / جوهرى ز آن كيميا گر شد بيار
entries.english: Regard, also, the practice of alchemy as the form; if a substance is produced by that alchemy, bring.

entries.farsi: جوهرى ز آن كيميا گر شد بيار / زين عرض جوهر همى‏زايد صفا
entries.english: Polishing is the form, O prince; from this cause is born the result, purity.

entries.farsi: پس مگو كه من عملها كرده‏ام / ‏دخل آن اعراض را بنما مرم
entries.english: Do not say, then, ‘I have done deeds’; show the income of those causes, do not evade.

entries.farsi: اين صفت كردن عرض باشد خمش / ‏سايه‏ى بز را پى قربان مكش
entries.english: This attribution of qualities is a cause. Be silent: do not sacrifice the goat’s shadow!”

entries.farsi: گفت شاها بى‏قنوط عقل نيست / ‏گر تو فرمايى عرض را نقل نيست
entries.english: The slave said, “O King, the mind cannot but despair if you say that causes are not carried over.

entries.farsi: پادشاها جز كه ياس بنده نيست / ‏گر عرض كان رفت باز آينده نيست
entries.english: O King, there is nothing but despair for the servant, if the cause that has gone is not coming back.

entries.farsi: گر نبودى مر عرض را نقل و حشر / فعل بودى باطل و اقوال فشر
entries.english: If there were no carrying over and resurrection of causes, action would be vain and words babble.

entries.farsi: اين عرضها نقل شد لونى دگر / حشر هر فانى بود كونى دگر
entries.english: These accidents are carried over in another guise: the resurrection of everything mortal is another existence.

entries.farsi: نقل هر چيزى بود هم اليقش / نقل هر چيزى بود هم اليقش
entries.english: The carrying over of everything is just as befits it: what befits the herd is its shepherd.

entries.farsi: وقت محشر هر عرض را صورتى است / ‏صورت هر يك عرض را نوبتى است
entries.english: At the time of the Resurrection every cause has an effect, and the cause of every result has a turn.

entries.farsi: بنگر اندر خود نه تو بودى عرض / ‏جنبش جفتى و جفتى با غرض
entries.english: Look on yourself. Were you not a result— the movement of copulation, and copulation with a purpose?

entries.farsi: آن فالن خانه كه ما ديديم خوش / بود موزون صفه و سقف و درش
entries.english: Look on houses and edifices: they were as tales in the architect.

entries.farsi: بنگر اندر خانه و كاشانه‏ها / در مهندس بود چون افسانه‏ها
entries.english: Such-and-such a house, which seemed to us beautiful, of which the hall, roof, and door were well-proportioned

entries.farsi: از مهندس آن عرض و انديشه‏ها / آلت آورد و ستون از بيشه‏ها
entries.english: It was the thought and idea from the architect brought the tools and pillars from handicrafts.

entries.farsi: چيست اصل و مايه‏ى هر پيشه‏اى / ‏جز خيال و جز عرض و انديشه‏اى
entries.english: What but some thought, result and idea is the origin and source of every handicraft?

entries.farsi: جمله اجزاى جهان را بى‏غرض / ‏درنگر حاصل نشد جز از عرض
entries.english: Look disinterestedly on all the parts of the world: they are not the result of except thought.

entries.farsi: اول فكر آخر آمد در عمل / ‏بنيت عالم چنان دان در ازل
entries.english: The beginning, which is thought, comes to an end in action; know that in such wise was the construction of the world in eternity.

entries.farsi: ميوه‏ها در فكر دل اول بود / در عمل ظاهر به آخر مى‏شود
entries.english: The fruits are first in the mind’s thought, at the last do they become manifest actually:

entries.farsi: چون عمل كردى شجر بنشاندى / ‏اندر آخر حرف اول خواندى
entries.english: When you have done work, planted the tree— at the end you read the first words.

entries.farsi: گر چه شاخ و برگ و بيخش اول است / ‏آن همه از بهر ميوه مرسل است
entries.english: Although its boughs, leaves, and roots are first, all those are sent for the sake of the fruit.

entries.farsi: پس سرى كه مغز آن افلاک بود / اندر آخر خواجه‏ى لوالك بود
entries.english: Therefore that hidden Thought which was the brain of those heavens was in the end the lord of lawlak.

entries.farsi: نقل اعراض است اين بحث و مقال / ‏نقل اعراض است اين شير و شگال
entries.english: This discussion and talk are the carrying over of causes; this lion and jackal are the carrying over of causes.

entries.farsi: جمله عالم خود عرض بودند تا / ‏اندر اين معنى بيامد هَلْ أتى
entries.english: Indeed, all created beings were ideas, so that in this sense was revealed—did there not come...?

entries.farsi: اين عرضها از چه زايد از صور / وين صور هم از چه زايد از فكر
entries.english: From where do those causes arise? From ideas. And from where arise these ideas? From thoughts.

entries.farsi: اين جهان يك فكرت است از عقل كل / ‏عقل چون شاه است و صورتها رسل
entries.english: This world is one thought from the Universal Intellect: the Intellect is like a king and the ideas envoys.

entries.farsi: عالم اول جهان امتحان / ‏عالم ثانى جزاى اين و آن
entries.english: The first world is the world of testing; the second world is the reward for this and that.

entries.farsi: چاكرت شاها جنايت مى‏كند / آن عرض زنجير و زندان مى‏شود
entries.english: Your servant, O King, commits a sin: that cause becomes chains and prison.

entries.farsi: بنده‏ات چون خدمت شايسته كرد / آن عرض نه خلعتى شد در نبرد
entries.english: When your slave performed goodly service, did not that action become a robe of honour in the battle?

entries.farsi: اين عرض با جوهر آن بيضه است و طير / اين از آن و آن از اين زايد به سير
entries.english: This cause and effect is like the chicken and the egg: this is produced by that and that by this, in succession.”

entries.farsi: گفت شاهنشه چنين گير المراد / اين عرضهاى تو يك جوهر نزاد
entries.english: The King said, “Take the meaning so: have these actions of yours not produced any result?”

entries.farsi: ز انكه گر پيدا شدى اشكال فكر / كافر و مومن نگفتى جز كه ذكر
entries.english: “Wisdom,” replied the slave, “has kept it concealed, in order that this world of good and evil may be a mystery,

entries.farsi: گفت مخفى داشته ست آن را خرد / تا بود غيب اين جهان نيك و بد
entries.english: Because, if the forms of thought were to become manifest, infidel and believer would speak nothing but praise.

entries.farsi: كى درين عالم بت و بتگر بدى / ‏چون كسى را زهره‏ى تسخر بدى
entries.english: Then, were this clearly seen, O King, not hidden, and were the mark of religion or infidelity on the forehead,

entries.farsi: پس عيان بودى نه غيب اى شاه اين / ‏نقش دين و كفر بودى بر جبين
entries.english: How would there be idol and idolater in this world? How would any one have the stomach to mock?

entries.farsi: پس قيامت بودى اين دنياى ما / در قيامت كى كند جرم و خطا
entries.english: Then this world of ours would be the Resurrection: who commits sin and wrong at the Resurrection?”

entries.farsi: گفت شه پوشيد حق پاداش بد / ليك از عامه نه از خاصان خود
entries.english: The King said, “God has veiled the retribution of evil, but from the vulgar, not from His own elect.

entries.farsi: گر به دامى افكنم من يك امير / از اميران خفيه دارم نه از وزير
entries.english: If I entrap one Amir, I keep it hidden from the Amirs, not from the Vizier.

entries.farsi: حق به من بنمود پس پاداش كار / وز صورهاى عملها صد هزار
entries.english: God, then, has shown to me the retribution of work and myriads of the forms of actions.

entries.farsi: تو نشانى ده كه من دانم تمام / ‏ماه را بر من نمى‏پوشد غمام
entries.english: Give a sign, for I know all: the cloud does not veil the moon from me.”

entries.farsi: گفت پس از گفت من مقصود چيست / ‏چون تو مى‏دانى كه آن چه بود چيست
entries.english: The slave said, “Then what is the object of my speaking, since you know what is that which has been?”

entries.farsi: گفت شه حكمت در اظهار جهان / ‏آن كه دانسته برون آيد عيان
entries.english: The King said, “The wisdom in making the world manifest that the known should come forth plainly.

entries.farsi: آن چه مى‏دانست تا پيدا نكرد / بر جهان ننهاد رنج طلق و درد
entries.english: Until He made visible that which He knew, He did not lay the pain of childbirth upon the world.

entries.farsi: يك زمان بى‏كار نتوانى نشست / ‏تا بدى يا نيكيى از تو نجست
entries.english: You cannot sit inactive for one moment: till some badness or goodness has issued from you.

entries.farsi: اين تقاضاهاى كار از بهر آن / ‏شد موكل تا شود سرت عيان
entries.english: These demands for action were appointed in order that your inward consciousness should come clearly into view.

entries.farsi: پس كالبه‏ى تن كجا ساكن شود / چون سر رشته‏ى ضميرش مى‏كشد
entries.english: How, then, should the reel, which is the body, become still, when the thread’s end, which is the mind, is pulling it?

entries.farsi: تاسه‏ى تو شد نشان آن كشش / بر تو بى‏كارى بود چون جان كنش
entries.english: The sign of that pulling is your anguish: to be inactive is to you like the death-agony.

entries.farsi: اين جهان و آن جهان زايد ابد / هر سبب مادر اثر از وى ولد
entries.english: This world and that world are for ever giving birth: every cause is a mother, the effect is born a child.

entries.farsi: چون اثر زاييد آن هم شد سبب / ‏تا بزايد او اثرهاى عجب
entries.english: When the effect was born, that too became a cause, so that it might give birth to wondrous effects.

entries.farsi: اين سببها نسل بر نسل است ليك / ‏ديده‏اى بايد منور نيك نيك
entries.english: These causes are generation on generation, but it needs a very well illumined eye.”

entries.farsi: شاه با او در سخن اينجا رسيد / يا بديد از وى نشانى يا نديد
entries.english: The King, in conversation with him, arrived at this point, that he saw from him a sign not apparent.

entries.farsi: گر بديد آن شاه جويا دور نيست / ‏ليك ما را ذكر آن دستور نيست
entries.english: If that searching King saw, ‘tis not strange; but we are not permitted to mention it.

entries.farsi: چون ز گرمابه بيامد آن غالم / ‏سوى خويشش خواند آن شاه و همام
entries.english: When that slave came from the warm bath, that King and lofty personage called him to his presence,

entries.farsi: گفت صحا لك نعيم دايم / بس لطيفى و ظريف و خوب رو
entries.english: “Health! Lasting happiness be yours!’ You are very fine and elegant and good-looking.

entries.farsi: اى دريغا گر نبودى در تو آن / ‏كه همى‏گويد براى تو فالن
entries.english: Oh, alas! If there were not in you that which so-and-so says about you?

entries.farsi: شاد گشتى هر كه رويت ديده‏يى / ‏ديدنت ملك جهان ارزيديى
entries.english: Whoever beheld your face would become glad; the sight of you would be worth the empire of the world.”

entries.farsi: گفت رمزى ز آن بگو اى پادشاه / ‏كز براى من بگفت آن دين تباه
entries.english: He said, “O King, utter some hint of what that miscreant said about me.”

entries.farsi: گفت اول وصف دو روييت كرد / كاشكارا تو دوايى خفيه درد
entries.english: The King said, “In the first place he described you as double-faced, saying that you are ostensibly a remedy secretly a disease.”

entries.farsi: خبث يارش را چو از شه گوش كرد / در زمان درياى خشمش جوش كرد
entries.english: When he heard from the King the malice of his companion, at once the sea of his anger surged up.

entries.farsi: كف بر آورد آن غالم و سرخ گشت / ‏تا كه موج هجو او از حد گذشت
entries.english: That slave foamed and reddened, so that the billows of his anger exceeded all bounds.

entries.farsi: كاو ز اول دم كه با من يار بود / همچو سگ در قحط بس گه خوار بود
entries.english: He said, “From the first moment that he was associated with me, he was a great eater of dung, like a dog in famine.”

entries.farsi: چون دمادم كرد هجوش چون جرس / ‏دست بر لب زد شهنشاهش كه بس
entries.english: As he satirised him moment after moment, like a bell, the King put his hand on his lips, saying, “Enough!”

entries.farsi: گفت دانستم ترا از وى بدان / ‏از تو جان گنده ست و از يارت دهان
entries.english: He said, “ I know you from him by that: in you the spirit is foul, and in your companion the mouth.

entries.farsi: پس نشين اى گنده جان از دور تو / تا امير او باشد و مأمور تو
entries.english: Therefore sit far off, O foul-spirited one, that he may be the commander and you under his command.”

entries.farsi: در حديث آمد كه تسبيح از ريا / همچو سبزه‏ى گولخن دان اى كيا
entries.english: It is in the Hadith: “Know, sir, that glorification from hypocrisy is like the verdure on a garbage heap.”

entries.farsi: ور بود صورت حقير و ناپذير / چون بود خلقش نكو در پاش مير
entries.english: Know, then, that a fair and goodly form with bad qualities is not worth a penny;

entries.farsi: پس بدان كه صورت خوب و نكو / با خصال بد نيرزد يك تسو
entries.english: And though the form is despicable and unpleasing, when his disposition is good, die at his feet!

entries.farsi: صورت ظاهر فنا گردد بدان / ‏عالم معنى بماند جاودان
entries.english: Know that the outward form passes away, the world of reality remains for ever.

entries.farsi: چند بازى عشق با نقش سبو / بگذر از نقش سبو رو آب جو
entries.english: How long will you play at loving the shape of the jug? Leave the shape of the jug; go, seek the water.

entries.farsi: صورتش ديدى ز معنى غافلى / ‏از صدف درى گزين گر عاقلى
entries.english: You have seen its form, you are unaware of the reality; pick out from the shell a pearl, if you are wise.

entries.farsi: اين صدفهاى قوالب در جهان / ‏گر چه جمله زنده‏اند از بحر جان
entries.english: These shells of bodies in the world, though they all are living by the Sea of Soul

entries.farsi: ليك اندر هر صدف نبود گهر / چشم بگشا در دل هر يك نگر
entries.english: Yet there is not a pearl in every shell: open your eyes and look into the heart of each one,

entries.farsi: كان چه دارد وين چه دارد مى‏گزين / ‏ز انكه كمياب است آن در ثمين
entries.english: And pick out what that one has and what this, because that costly pearl is seldom found.

entries.farsi: گر به صورت مى‏روى كوهى به شكل / در بزرگى هست صد چندان كه لعل
entries.english: If you go to the form, by external appearance a mountain is a hundred times as much as a ruby in bigness;

entries.farsi: هم به صورت دست و پا و پشم تو / هست صد چندان كه نقش چشم تو
entries.english: Also, in respect of form, your hands and feet and hair are a hundred times as much as the contour of the eye;

entries.farsi: ليك پوشيده نباشد بر تو اين / ‏كز همه اعضا دو چشم آمد گزين
entries.english: But this is not hidden from you, that the two eyes are the choicest of all members.

entries.farsi: از يك انديشه كه آيد در درون / ‏صد جهان گردد به يك دم سر نگون
entries.english: By one thought that comes into the mind a hundred worlds are overturned in a single moment.

entries.farsi: جسم سلطان گر به صورت يك بود / صد هزاران لشكرش در پى دود
entries.english: If the body of the Sultan is, in form, one, hundreds of thousands of soldiers run behind.

entries.farsi: باز شكل و صورت شاه صفى / ‏هست محكوم يكى فكر خفى
entries.english: Again, the figure and form of the excellent King are ruled by one invisible thought.

entries.farsi: خلق بى‏پايان ز يك انديشه بين / ‏گشته چون سيلى روانه بر زمين
entries.english: Behold people without end who, moved by one thought, have gone over the earth like a flood;

entries.farsi: هست آن انديشه پيش خلق خرد / ليك چون سيلى جهان را خورد و برد
entries.english: Small is that thought in the people’s eyes, but like a flood it swallowed and swept away the world.

entries.farsi: پس چو مى‏بينى كه از انديشه‏اى / ‏قايم است اندر جهان هر پيشه‏اى
entries.english: So, when you see that from a thought every craft in the world subsists

entries.farsi: خانه‏ها و قصرها و شهرها / كوهها و دشتها و نهرها
entries.english: Houses and palaces and cities, mountains, plains and rivers,

entries.farsi: هم زمين و بحر و هم مهر و فلك / ‏زنده از وى همچو كز دريا سمك
entries.english: Earth and ocean as well as sun and sky, are living from it as fishes from the sea

entries.farsi: پس چرا از ابلهى پيش تو كور / تن سليمان است و انديشه چو مور
entries.english: Then why in your foolishness, O blind one, does the body seem to you a Solomon, and thought as an ant?

entries.farsi: مى‏نمايد پيش چشمت كه بزرگ / ‏هست انديشه چو موش و كوه گرگ
entries.english: To your eye the mountain appears great: thought is like a mouse, and the mountain a wolf.

entries.farsi: عالم اندر چشم تو هول و عظيم / ‏ز ابر و رعد و چرخ دارى لرز و بيم
entries.english: The world in your eyes is awful and sublime: you tremble and are frightened at the clouds and the thunder and the sky,

entries.farsi: وز جهان فكرتى اى كم ز خر / ايمن و غافل چو سنگ بى‏خبر
entries.english: While in regard to the world of thought, O less than the ass, you are secure and indifferent as a witless stone,

entries.farsi: ز انكه نقشى وز خرد بى‏بهره‏اى / ‏آدمى خو نيستى خر كره‏اى
entries.english: Because you are a shape and have no portion of intelligence; you are not of human nature, you are an ass’s colt.

entries.farsi: سايه را تو شخص مى‏بينى ز جهل / ‏شخص از آن شد نزد تو بازى و سهل
entries.english: From ignorance you deem the shadow to be the kernel: hence to you the essence has become a plaything and of slight account.

entries.farsi: باش تا روزى كه آن فكر و خيال / ‏بر گشايد بى‏حجابى پر و بال
entries.english: Wait till the Day when that thought and imagining unfolds its wings and pinions without any veil.

entries.farsi: كوهها بينى شده چون پشم نرم / ‏نيست گشته اين زمين سرد و گرم
entries.english: You will see that the mountains have become soft like wool, this Earth of hot and cold have become nothing;

entries.farsi: نه سما بينى نه اختر نه وجود / جز خداى واحد حى ودود
entries.english: You will see neither the sky, the stars nor existence only God, the One, the Living, the Loving.

entries.farsi: يك فسانه راست آمد يا دروغ / ‏تا دهد مر راستيها را فروغ
entries.english: Here is a tale, true or false, to illustrate truths.

sections.title_fa: حسد كردن حشم بر غالم خاص
sections.title_en: How the retainers envied the favourite slave.

entries.farsi: پادشاهى بنده‏اى را از كرم / ‏بر گزيده بود بر جمله حشم
entries.english: A King had, of his grace, preferred a certain slave above all his retinue.

entries.farsi: جامگى او وظيفه‏ى چل امير / ده يك قدرش نديدى صد وزير
entries.english: His allowance was the stipend of forty Amirs; a hundred Viziers would not see a tenth of its amount.

entries.farsi: از كمال طالع و اقبال و بخت / ‏او ايازى بود و شه محمود وقت
entries.english: Through the perfection of natal star and prosperity and fortune he was an Ayaz, while the King was the Mafirmid of the time.

entries.farsi: روح او با روح شه در اصل خويش / ‏پيش از اين تن بوده هم پيوند و خويش
entries.english: His spirit in its origin, before this body, was Close to and akin to the King’s spirit.

entries.farsi: كار آن دارد كه پيش از تن بده ست / ‏بگذر از اينها كه نو حادث شده ست
entries.english: That matters which has existed before the body; leave these things which have newly sprung into being.

entries.farsi: آن چه گندم كاشتندش و آن چه جو / چشم او آن جاست روز و شب گرو
entries.english: That which matters belongs to the knower, for he is not squinting: his eye is upon the things first sown.

entries.farsi: كار عارف راست كاو نه احول است / ‏چشم او بر كشتهاى اول است
entries.english: What was sown as wheat or as barley— day and night his eye is fastened on that place.

entries.farsi: آنچ آبست است شب جز آن نزاد / حيله‏ها و مكرها باد است باد
entries.english: Night gave birth to nothing but what she was pregnant with: designs and plots are wind, wind.

entries.farsi: كى كند دل خوش به حيلتهاى گش / ‏آن كه بيند حيله‏ى حق بر سرش
entries.english: How should he please his heart with fair designs who sees the design of God over them?

entries.farsi: او درون دام دامى مى‏نهد / جان تو نه اين جهد نه آن جهد
entries.english: He is laying a snare within the snare: by your life, neither this will escape nor will that.

entries.farsi: گر برويد ور بريزد صد گياه / ‏عاقبت بر رويد آن كشته‏ى اله
entries.english: Though a hundred herbs grow and fade, there will grow up at last that which God has sown.

entries.farsi: كشت نو كاريد بر كشت نخست / ‏اين دوم فانى است و آن اول درست
entries.english: He sowed new seed over the first seed; this second is passing away, and the first is sound.

entries.farsi: تخم اول كامل و بگزيده است / ‏تخم ثانى فاسد و پوسيده است
entries.english: The first seed is perfect and choice; the second seed is corrupt and rotten.

entries.farsi: افكن اين تدبير خود را پيش دوست / ‏گر چه تدبيرت هم از تدبير اوست
entries.english: Cast away this contrivance of yours before the Beloved— though your contrivance indeed is of His contriving.

entries.farsi: كار آن دارد كه حق افراشته ست / آخر آن رويد كه اول كاشته ست
entries.english: That which God has raised has use: what He has at first sown at last grows.

entries.farsi: هر چه كارى از براى او بكار / چون اسير دوستى اى دوستدار
entries.english: Whatever you sow, sow for His sake, inasmuch as you are the Beloved’s captive, O lover.

entries.farsi: گرد نفس دزد و كار او مپيچ / ‏هر چه آن نه كار حق هيچ است هيچ
entries.english: Do not hang about the thievish fleshly soul and its work: whatsoever is not God’s work is nothing, nothing.

entries.farsi: پيش از آن كه روز دين پيدا شود / نزد مالك دزد شب رسوا شود
entries.english: Before the Day of Resurrection shall appear and the thief be shamed before Him whose is the Kingdom,

entries.farsi: رخت دزديده به تدبير و فنش / ‏مانده روز داورى بر گردنش
entries.english: With the goods stolen by his contrivance and craft remaining on his neck at the Day of Judgment.

entries.farsi: صد هزاران عقل با هم بر جهند / تا به غير دام او دامى نهند
entries.english: Hundreds of thousands of minds may jump together to lay a snare other than His snare;

entries.farsi: دام خود را سخت‏تر يابند و بس / ‏كى نمايد قوتى با باد خس
entries.english: They only find their snare more grievous, how can straws show any power against the wind?

entries.farsi: گر تو گويى فايده‏ى هستى چه بود / در سؤالت فايده هست اى عنود
entries.english: If you say, “What was the profit of being?”, There is profit in your question, O stubborn one

entries.farsi: ور سؤالت را بسى فاييده‏هاست / ‏پس جهان بى‏فايده آخر چراست
entries.english: If this question of yours has no profit, why should I listen to it in vain and fruitlessly?

entries.farsi: گر ندارد اين سؤالت فايده / ‏چه شنويم اين را عبث بى‏عايده
entries.english: And if there are many profits in your question, then why, pray, is the world unprofitable?

entries.farsi: فايده‏ى تو گر مرا فاييده نيست / ‏مر ترا چون فايده ست از وى مه ايست
entries.english: And, if from one standpoint the world is unprofitable, from other standpoints it is advantageous.

entries.farsi: ور جهان از يك جهت بى‏فايده ست / ‏از جهتهاى دگر پر عايده ست
entries.english: If your profit is no profit to me, since it is a profit to you, do not withdraw from it.”

entries.farsi: حسن يوسف عالمى را فايده / ‏گر چه بر اخوان عبث بد زايده
entries.english: The beauty of Joseph profited a world, though to his brethren it was superfluous.

entries.farsi: لحن داودى چنان محبوب بود / ليك بر محروم بانگ چوب بود
entries.english: The melodies of David were so dear, but to the banished they were the noise of wood.

entries.farsi: آب نيل از آب حيوان بد فزون / ‏ليك بر محروم و منكر بود خون
entries.english: The water of the Nile was superior to the Water of Life, but to the shunned and unbelieving it was blood.

entries.farsi: هست بر مومن شهيدى زندگى / ‏بر منافق مردن است و ژندگى
entries.english: To the true believer martyrdom is life; to the hypocrite it is death and corruption.

entries.farsi: چيست در عالم بگو يك نعمتى / ‏كه نه محرومند از وى امتى
entries.english: Tell, what single blessing is there in the world, from which some group of people is not excluded?

entries.farsi: گاو و خر را فايده چه در شكر / هست هر جان را يكى قوتى دگر
entries.english: What profits have the ox and the ass in sugar? Every soul has a different food;

entries.farsi: ليك گر آن قوت بر وى عارضى است / ‏پس نصيحت كردن او را رايضى است
entries.english: But if that food is accidental to it then admonition is the correction for it.

entries.farsi: چون كسى كاو از مرض گل داشت دوست / ‏گر چه پندارد كه آن خود قوت اوست
entries.english: As one who from disease has become fond of clay— though he may suppose that that is indeed his food,

entries.farsi: قوت اصلى را فرامش كرده است / ‏روى در قوت مرض آورده است
entries.english: He has forgotten his original food and has taken himself to eating the food of disease.

entries.farsi: نوش را بگذاشته سم خورده است / ‏قوت علت همچو چوبش كرده است
entries.english: Having given up honey, he has eaten poison; he has made the food of disease as fat.

entries.farsi: قوت اصلى بشر نور خداست / ‏قوت حيوانى مر او را ناسزاست
entries.english: Man’s original food is the Light of God: animal food is improper for him;

entries.farsi: ليك از علت در اين افتاد دل / ‏كه خورد او روز و شب زين آب و گل
entries.english: But, in consequence of disease, his mind has fallen into this , that day and night he should eat of this water and clay.

entries.farsi: روى زرد و پاى سست و دل سبك / ‏كو غذاى و السما ذات الحبك
entries.english: Pale-faced, weak-footed, faint-hearted— where is the food of by Heaven which hath tracks?

entries.farsi: آن غذاى خاصگان دولت است / ‏خوردن آن بى‏گلو و آلت است
entries.english: That is the food of the chosen ones of the sovereignty; the eating of which is without throat or instrument.

entries.farsi: شد غذاى آفتاب از نور عرش / مر حسود و ديو را از دود فرش
entries.english: The food of the sun is from the light of the Throne; the envious and devilish is from the smoke of the carpet.

entries.farsi: در شهيدان يُرْزَقُونَ فرمود حق / ‏آن غذا را نه دهان بد نه طبق
entries.english: God said concerning the martyrs, they are receiving sustenance. For that food there was neither mouth nor dish.

entries.farsi: دل ز هر يارى غذايى مى‏خورد / دل ز هر علمى صفايى مى‏برد
entries.english: The heart is eating a food from every single companion; the heart is getting a excellence from every single knowledge.

entries.farsi: صورت هر آدمى چون كاسه‏اى است / ‏چشم از معنى او حساسه‏اى است
entries.english: Every human being’s form is like a cup; the eye is aware of his reality.

entries.farsi: از لقاى هر كسى چيزى خورى / ‏و ز قران هر قرين چيزى برى
entries.english: You eat something from meeting with any one, and you carry away something from conjunction with any associate.

entries.farsi: چون ستاره با ستاره شد قرين / ‏اليق هر دو اثر زايد يقين
entries.english: When planet comes into conjunction with planet, the effect appropriate to them both is assuredly produced,

entries.farsi: چون قران مرد و زن زايد بشر / وز قران سنگ و آهن شد شرر
entries.english: As the conjunction of man and woman brings to birth the human being, and sparks arise from the conjunction of stone and iron;

entries.farsi: و ز قران خاك با بارانها / ميوه‏ها و سبزه و ريحانها
entries.english: And from the conjunction of earth with rains fruits and greenery and sweet herbs;

entries.farsi: و ز قران سبزه‏ها با آدمى / ‏دل خوشى و بى‏غمى و خرمى
entries.english: And from the conjunction of green things with man joy of heart and carelessness and happiness;

entries.farsi: وز قران خرمى با جان ما / مى‏بزايد خوبى و احسان ما
entries.english: And from the conjunction of happiness with our souls are born our goodness and beneficence.

entries.farsi: قابل خوردن شود اجسام ما / چون بر آيد از تفرج كام ما
entries.english: Our bodies become capable of eating and drinking when our desire for recreation is satisfied.

entries.farsi: سرخ رويى از قران خون بود / خون ز خورشيد خوش گلگون بود
entries.english: Redness of countenance is from the conjunction of blood; blood is from the beautiful rose-coloured sun.

entries.farsi: بهترين رنگها سرخى بود / و آن ز خورشيد است و از وى مى‏رسد
entries.english: Redness is the best of colours, and that is of the sun and is arriving from it.

entries.farsi: هر زمينى كان قرين شد با زحل / ‏شوره گشت و كشت را نبود محل
entries.english: Every land that has been conjoined with Saturn has become sour and is not the place for sowing.

entries.farsi: قوت اندر فعل آيد ز اتفاق / ‏چون قران ديو با اهل نفاق
entries.english: Through concurrence power comes into action, as the conjunction of the Devil with hypocrites.

entries.farsi: اين معانى راست از چرخ نهم / ‏بى‏همه طاق و طرم طاق و طرم
entries.english: These spiritual truths without any pomp and grandeur have pomp and grandeur from the Ninth Heaven.

entries.farsi: خلق را طاق و طرم عاريت است / ‏امر را طاق و طرم ماهيت است
entries.english: The pomp and grandeur belonging to creation is a borrowed thing; the pomp and grandeur belonging to the Command is an essential thing

entries.farsi: از پى طاق و طرم خوارى كشند / بر اميد عز در خوارى خوشند
entries.english: For the sake of pomp and grandeur they endure abasement; in the hope of glory they are happy in abasement.

entries.farsi: بر اميد عز ده روزه‏ى خدوك / ‏گردن خود كرده‏اند از غم چو دوك
entries.english: In the hope of a ten days glory of annoyance, they have made their necks, from anxiety, as a spindle.

entries.farsi: چون نمى‏آيند اينجا كه منم / ‏كاندر اين عز آفتاب روشنم
entries.english: How do not they come to this place where I am?— for in this glory I am the shining Sun.

entries.farsi: مشرق او نسبت ذرات او / نه بر آمد نه فرو شد ذات او
entries.english: The rising-place of the sun is the pitch-coloured tower, this Sun is beyond rising places.

entries.farsi: مشرق خورشيد برج قيرگون / ‏آفتاب ما ز مشرقها برون
entries.english: His “rising-place” in relation to His specks: His essence neither rose nor set.

entries.farsi: ما كه واپس ماند ذرات وى‏ايم / ‏در دو عالم آفتابى بى‏فى‏ايم
entries.english: I who am left behind by His motes am in both worlds a sun without shadow.

entries.farsi: باز گرد شمس مى‏گردم عجب / ‏هم ز فر شمس باشد اين سبب
entries.english: Still, I am revolving round the Sun—it is wonderful; the cause of this is the majesty of the Sun.

entries.farsi: شمس باشد بر سببها مطلع / ‏هم از او حبل سببها منقطع
entries.english: The Sun is acquainted with causes; at the same time the cord of causes is severed from Him.

entries.farsi: صد هزاران بار ببريدم اميد / از كه از شمس اين شما باور كنيد
entries.english: Hundreds of thousands of times have I cut off hope—of whom? Of the Sun? Do you believe this?

entries.farsi: تو مرا باور مكن كز آفتاب / ‏صبر دارم من و يا ماهى ز آب
entries.english: Do not believe of me that I can endure to be without the Sun, or the fish to be without water;

entries.farsi: ور شوم نوميد نوميدى من / ‏عين صنع آفتاب است اى حسن
entries.english: And if I become despairing, my despair is the objective manifestation of the Sun’s work, O goodly.

entries.farsi: عين صنع از نفس صانع چون برد / هيچ هست از غير هستى چون چرد
entries.english: How should the objective manifestation of the work be cut off from the very self of the Worker? How should any object of being pasture on aught but Being?

entries.farsi: جمله هستيها از اين روضه چرند / گر براق و تازيان ور خود خرند
entries.english: All beings pasture on this Meadow, whether they be Buraq or Arab horses or even asses;

entries.farsi: و انكه گردشها از آن دريا نديد / هر دم آرد رو به صحرايى جديد
entries.english: And he that has not regarded occurrences as from that Sea, at every instant turns his face towards a new point of orientation.

entries.farsi: او ز بحر عذب آب شور خورد / تا كه آب شور او را كور كرد
entries.english: He has drunk salt water from the sweet Sea, so that the salt water has made him blind.

entries.farsi: بحر مى‏گويد به دست راست خور / ز آب من اى كور تا يابى بصر
entries.english: The Sea is saying, “Drink of my water with the right hand, O blind one, that you may gain sight.”

entries.farsi: هست دست راست اينجا ظن راست / ‏كاو بداند نيك و بد را كز كجاست
entries.english: Here “the right hand” is right opinion, which knows concerning good and evil where they are.

entries.farsi: نيزه گردانى است اى نيزه كه تو / راست مى‏گردى گهى گاهى دو تو
entries.english: O lance, there is a Lancer’, so that sometimes you become straight, sometimes double.

entries.farsi: ما ز عشق شمس دين بى‏ناخنيم / ‏ور نه ما آن كور را بينا كنيم
entries.english: Through love of Shams-i Din I am without claws; else would not I make this blind one see?

entries.farsi: هان ضياء الحق حسام الدين تو زود / داروش كن كورى چشم حسود
entries.english: Listen, O Light of the Truth, Husamu’ddin, speedily heal him, to the confusion of the eye of the envious;

entries.farsi: توتياى كبرياى تيز فعل / ‏داروى ظلمت كش استيز فعل
entries.english: The quick-acting polishing powder of majesty, the darkness-killing remedy of the recalcitrant,

entries.farsi: آن كه گر بر چشم اعمى بر زند / ظلمت صد ساله را زو بر كند
entries.english: Which, if it strikes on the eye of the blind man, it will dispel from him a hundred years’ darkness.

entries.farsi: جمله كوران را دوا كن جز حسود / كز حسودى بر تو مى‏آرد جحود
entries.english: Heal all the blind ones except the envious man who from envy is bringing denial against thee.

entries.farsi: مر حسودت را اگر چه آن منم / ‏جان مده تا همچنين جان مى‏كنم
entries.english: To your envier, though it be I, do not give life, so that I may be suffering the agony of death even as he is.

entries.farsi: آن كه او باشد حسود آفتاب / ‏و انكه مى‏رنجد ز بود آفتاب
entries.english: The one that is envious of the Sun and the one that is fretting at the existence of the Sun.

entries.farsi: اينت درد بى‏دوا كاو راست آه / ‏اينت افتاده ابد در قعر چاه
entries.english: Look, this is the incurable disease which he has, alas; look, this is one fallen for ever to the bottom of the pit.

entries.farsi: نفى خورشيد ازل بايست او / كى بر آيد اين مراد او بگو
entries.english: What he wants is the extinction of the Sun of eternity. Tell, how should this desire of his come to pass?

entries.farsi: باز آن باشد كه باز آيد به شاه / ‏باز كور است آن كه شد گم كرده راه
entries.english: The falcon is he that comes back to the King; he that has lost the way is the blind falcon.

entries.farsi: راه را گم كرد و در ويران فتاد / باز در ويران بر جغدان فتاد
entries.english: It lost the way and fell into the wilderness; then in the wilderness it fell in with owls.

entries.farsi: او همه نور است از نور رضا / ليك كورش كرد سرهنگ قضا
entries.english: It is wholly light from the Light of approval, but the marshal, Fate, blinded it.

entries.farsi: خاك در چشمش زد و از راه برد / در ميان جغد و ويرانش سپرد
entries.english: He threw dust in its eyes and took it away from the road; he left it amidst owls and the wilderness.

entries.farsi: بر سرى جغدانش بر سر مى‏زنند / پر و بال نازنينش مى‏كنند
entries.english: To crown all, the owls attack it and tear its lovely wing-feathers and plumes.

entries.farsi: ولوله افتاد در جغدان كه ها / باز آمد تا بگيرد جاى ما
entries.english: A clamour arose amongst the owls— “Ha! The falcon has come to seize our dwelling-place.”

entries.farsi: چون سگان كوى پر خشم و مهيب / ‏اندر افتادند در دلق غريب
entries.english: As the street-dogs, wrathful and terrifying, have fallen upon the frock of a stranger.

entries.farsi: باز گويد من چه در خوردم به جغد / صد چنين ويران فدا كردم به جغد
entries.english: “How am I fit,” says the falcon, “for owls? I give up to the owls a hundred wildernesses like this.

entries.farsi: من نخواهم بود اينجا مى‏روم / ‏سوى شاهنشاه راجع مى‏شوم
entries.english: I do not wish to stay here, I am going, I will return to the King of kings.

entries.farsi: اين خراب آباد در چشم شماست / ‏ور نه ما را ساعد شه باز جاست
entries.english: Do not kill yourselves, O owls, for I am not settling: I am going home.

entries.farsi: خويشتن مكشيد اى جغدان كه من / ‏نه مقيمم مى‏روم سوى وطن
entries.english: This ruin is a thriving abode in your eyes; for me, however, the King’s fore-arm is the place whither one returns.”

entries.farsi: جغد گفتا باز حيلت مى‏كند / تا ز خان و مان شما را بر كند
entries.english: The owl said, “The falcon is plotting to uproot you from house and home.

entries.farsi: خانه‏هاى ما بگيرد او به مكر / بر كند ما را به سالوسى ز وكر
entries.english: He will seize our houses by cunning; he will tear us out of our nests by hypocrisy.

entries.farsi: مى‏نمايد سيرى اين حيلت پرست / ‏و اهلل از جمله‏ى حريصان بدتر است
entries.english: This devotee of guile pretends to be perfectly satisfied; by God, he is worse than all the greedy together.

entries.farsi: او خورد از حرص طين را همچو دبس / ‏دنبه مسپاريد اى ياران به خرس
entries.english: From greediness he eats clay as date-syrup: O friends, do not entrust the sheep’s tail to the bear.

entries.farsi: الف از شه مى‏زند وز دست شاه / ‏تا برد او ما سليمان را ز راه
entries.english: He is boasting of the King and the King’s hand, in order that he may lead us astray, simple-minded as we are.

entries.farsi: خود چه جنس شاه باشد مرغكى / ‏مشنوش گر عقل دارى اندكى
entries.english: How, indeed, should a petty bird be the congener of the King? Do not listen to him, if you have a little understanding.

entries.farsi: جنس شاه است او و يا جنس وزير / هيچ باشد اليق لوزينه سير
entries.english: Is he the King’s or the Vizier’s congener? Is garlic at all suitable to baklava?

entries.farsi: آن چه مى‏گويد ز مكر و فعل و فن / ‏هست سلطان با حشم جوياى من
entries.english: His saying, from deceit and feint and artifice, ‘The King with his retinue is searching after me,’

entries.farsi: اينت ماليخولياى ناپذير / اينت الف خام و دام گول گير
entries.english: Here’s an absurd mad idea for you, here’s a vain boast and a snare to catch blockheads!

entries.farsi: هر كه اين باور كند از ابلهى است / ‏مرغك الغر چه در خورد شهى است
entries.english: Any one who believes this—it is because of foolishness: how is a slender little bird fit for royalty?

entries.farsi: كمترين جغد ار زند بر مغز او / مر و را يارى‏گرى از شاه كو
entries.english: If the smallest owl should strike at his brain, where is assistance for him from the King?”

entries.farsi: گفت باز ار يك پر من بشكند / بيخ جغدستان شهنشه بر كند
entries.english: The falcon said, “If a single feather of mine be broken, the King of kings will uproot the owlery.

entries.farsi: جغد چه بود خود اگر بازى مرا / دل برنجاند كند با من جفا
entries.english: What is an owl? Even if a falcon vex my heart and maltreat me,

entries.farsi: شه كند توده به هر شيب و فراز / صد هزاران خرمن از سرهاى باز
entries.english: The King will heap up in every lowland and highland hundreds of thousands of stacks of falcons’ heads.

entries.farsi: پاسبان من عنايات وى است / ‏هر كجا كه من روم شه در پى است
entries.english: His favour keeps watch over me; wherever I go, the King is behind.

entries.farsi: در دل سلطان خيال من مقيم / ‏بى‏خيال من دل سلطان سقيم
entries.english: My image is abiding in the King’s heart: sick the King’s heart without my image.

entries.farsi: چون بپراند مرا شه در روش / ‏مى‏پرم بر اوج دل چون پرتوش
entries.english: When the King bids me fly in His Way I fly up to the heart’s zenith, like His beams.

entries.farsi: همچو ماه و آفتابى مى‏پرم / ‏پرده‏هاى آسمانها مى‏درم
entries.english: I fly as a moon and sun; I tear the curtains of the skies.

entries.farsi: روشنى عقلها از فكرتم / ‏انفطار آسمان از فطرتم
entries.english: The light of intellects is from my thought; the bursting forth of heaven is from my original nature.

entries.farsi: بازم و حيران شود در من هما / جغد كه بود تا بداند سر ما
entries.english: I am a falcon, and the phoenix becomes lost in amazement at me: who is an owl, that it should know my secret?

entries.farsi: شه براى من ز زندان ياد كرد / صد هزاران بسته را آزاد كرد
entries.english: For my sake the King conceived of the prison, and set free hundreds of thousands of those in bondage.

entries.farsi: يك دمم با جغدها دمساز كرد / از دم من جغدها را باز كرد
entries.english: He made me familiar with the owls for a moment, and by means of my breath he made the owls falcons.

entries.farsi: اى خنك جغدى كه در پرواز من / ‏فهم كرد از نيك بختى راز من
entries.english: Oh, happy the owl that in my flight had the good fortune to apprehend my mystery.

entries.farsi: در من آويزيد تا نازان شويد / گر چه جغدانيد شهبازان شويد
entries.english: Cling to me, that you may become exultant, you may become royal falcons, although you are owls.

entries.farsi: آن كه باشد با چنان شاهى حبيب / ‏هر كجا افتد چرا باشد غريب
entries.english: He that is dear to such a King— wherever he lands, why should he be a stranger?

entries.farsi: هر كه باشد شاه دردش را دوا / گر چو نى نالد نباشد بى‏نوا
entries.english: Any one for whose pain the King is the remedy— though he wail like the Ney, he is not without plenty.

entries.farsi: مالك ملكم نيم من طبل خوار / طبل بازم مى‏زند شه از كنار
entries.english: I am the owner of the kingdom, I am not a sycophant: the King is beating the falcon-drum for me from the shore.

entries.farsi: طبل باز من نداى ارجعي / ‏حق گواه من به رغم مدعى
entries.english: My falcon-drum is the call, ‘Return!’ God is my witness in despite adversary.

entries.farsi: من نيم جنس شهنشه دور از او / ليك دارم در تجلى نور از او
entries.english: I am not a congener of the King of kings—far be it from Him!— but I have light from Him in self-manifestation.

entries.farsi: نيست جنسيت ز روى شكل و ذات / ‏آب جنس خاك آمد در نبات
entries.english: Homogeneity is not in respect of form and essence: water becomes homogeneous with earth in the plant.

entries.farsi: باد جنس آتش آمد در قوام / ‏طبع را جنس آمده ست آخر مدام
entries.english: Wind (air) becomes homogeneous with fire in consistency; wine at last becomes homogeneous with the constitution

entries.farsi: جنس ما چون نيست جنس شاه ما / ماى ما شد بهر ماى او فنا
entries.english: Since my genus is not the genus of my King, my ego has passed away for the sake of His ego.

entries.farsi: چون فنا شد ماى ما او ماند فرد / پيش پاى اسب او گردم چو گرد
entries.english: Inasmuch as my ego passed away, He remained alone: I roll at the feet of His horse, like dust.

entries.farsi: خاك شد جان و نشانيهاى او / هست بر خاكش نشان پاى او
entries.english: The soul became dust, and the signs of it are the mark of His feet on its dust.

entries.farsi: خاك پايش شو براى اين نشان / ‏تا شوى تاج سر گردن كشان
entries.english: Become dust at His feet for the sake of this mark, in order that you may become the crown on the head of the lofty.

entries.farsi: تا كه نفريبد شما را شكل من / ‏نقل من نوشيد پيش از نقل من
entries.english: Let not my form beguile you: partake of my dessert before my departure.”

entries.farsi: اى بسا كس را كه صورت راه زد / قصد صورت كرد و بر اهلل زد
entries.english: Oh, many a one whom the cause waylaid: he aimed at the cause and struck at God.

entries.farsi: آخر اين جان با بدن پيوسته است / ‏هيچ اين جان با بدن مانند هست
entries.english: After all, this soul is joined to the body, has this soul any likeness to the body?

entries.farsi: تاب نور چشم با پيه است جفت / ‏نور دل در قطره‏ى خونى نهفت
entries.english: The sparkle of the eye’s light is paired with the fat; the light of the heart is hidden in a drop of blood;

entries.farsi: شادى اندر گرده و غم در جگر / عقل چون شمعى درون مغز سر
entries.english: Joy in the kidneys, grief in the liver; intellect, as a candle, inside the brain in the head.

entries.farsi: اين تعلقها نه بى‏كيف است و چون / ‏عقلها در دانش چونى زبون
entries.english: These connections are not without a how and a why, as regards knowledge of the why minds are impotent.

entries.farsi: جان كل با جان جزو آسيب كرد / جان از او درى ستد در جيب كرد
entries.english: The Universal Soul came into contact with the partial soul, and soul received from it a pearl and put it into its bosom.

entries.farsi: همچو مريم جان از آن آسيب جيب / ‏حامله شد از مسيح دل فريب
entries.english: Through that touch on its bosom the soul became pregnant, like Mary, with a heart-beguiling Messiah,

entries.farsi: آن مسيحى نه كه بر خشك و تر است / ‏آن مسيحى كز مساحت برتر است
entries.english: Not the Messiah who is on land and water, the Messiah who is beyond measuring.

entries.farsi: پس ز جان جان چو حامل گشت جان / ‏از چنين جانى شود حامل جهان
entries.english: So when the soul has been impregnated by the Soul of soul, by such a soul the world is impregnated.

entries.farsi: پس جهان زايد جهان ديگرى / ‏اين حشر را وا نمايد محشرى
entries.english: Then the world gives birth to another world, and displays to this congregated people a place of congregation.

entries.farsi: تا قيامت گر بگويم بشمرم / ‏من ز شرح اين قيامت قاصرم
entries.english: Though I should speak and recount till the Resurrection, I lack the power to describe this resurrection.

entries.farsi: اين سخنها خود به معنى يا ربى است / ‏حرفها دام دم شيرين لبى است
entries.english: These sayings, indeed, are really an “O Lord”; the words are the lure for the breath of a sweet-lipped One.

entries.farsi: چون كند تقصير پس چون تن زند / چون كه لبيكش به يا رب مى‏رسد
entries.english: How, then, should he fail? How should he be silent, inasmuch as “Here am I” is coming in response to his “O Lord”?

entries.farsi: هست لبيكى كه نتوانى شنيد / ليك سر تا پاى بتوانى چشيد
entries.english: It is a “Here am I” that you cannot hear, but can taste from head to foot.

sections.title_fa: كلوخ انداختن تشنه از سر ديوار در جوى آب
sections.title_en: How the thirsty man threw bricks from the top of the wall

entries.farsi: بر لب جو بود ديوارى بلند / بر سر ديوار تشنه‏ى دردمند
entries.english: into the stream of water.

entries.farsi: مانعش از آب آن ديوار بود / از پى آب او چو ماهى زار بود
entries.english: On the bank of the stream there was a high wall, and on the top of the wall a sorrowful thirsty man.

entries.farsi: ناگهان انداخت او خشتى در آب / ‏بانگ آب آمد به گوشش چون خطاب
entries.english: The wall hindered him from the water; he was in distress for the water, like a fish.

entries.farsi: چون خطاب يار شيرين لذيذ / مست كرد آن بانگ آبش چون نبيذ
entries.english: Suddenly he threw a brick into the water: the noise of the water came to his ear like spoken words,

entries.farsi: از صفاى بانگ آب آن ممتحن / ‏گشت خشت انداز ز آن جا خشت‏كن
entries.english: Like words spoken by a sweet and delicious friend: the noise of the water made him drunken as wine.

entries.farsi: آب مى‏زد بانگ يعنى هى ترا / فايده چه زين زدن خشتى مرا
entries.english: From the pleasure of the noise of the water, that sorely tried man began to hurl and tear off bricks from that place.

entries.farsi: تشنه گفت آبا مرا دو فايده است / ‏من از اين صنعت ندارم هيچ دست
entries.english: The water was making a noise, that is to say, “Hey, what is the advantage to you of this hurling a brick at me?”

entries.farsi: فايده‏ى اول سماع بانگ آب / ‏كاو بود مر تشنگان را چون رباب
entries.english: The thirsty man said, “ O water, I have two advantages: I will in no way refrain from this work.

entries.farsi: بانگ او چون بانگ اسرافيل شد / مرده را زين زندگى تحويل شد
entries.english: The first advantage is hearing the noise of the water, which to thirsty men is as a rebeck.

entries.farsi: يا چو بانگ رعد ايام بهار / باغ مى‏يابد از او چندين نگار
entries.english: The noise thereof has become like the noise of Israfil: by this life has been transferred to one dead;
Or like the noise of thunder in days of spring— from it the garden obtains so many ornaments;

entries.farsi: يا چو بر درويش ايام زكات / ‏يا چو بر محبوس پيغام نجات
entries.english: Or like the days of alms to a poor man, or like the message of deliverance to a prisoner.

entries.farsi: چون دم رحمان بود كان از يمن / ‏مى‏رسد سوى محمد بى‏دهن
entries.english: It is like the breath of the Merciful which, without mouth, comes to Mohammed from Yemen;

entries.farsi: يا چو بوى احمد مرسل بود / كان به عاصى در شفاعت مى‏رسد
entries.english: Or it is like the scent of Ahmad, the Apostle, which in intercession comes to the sinner;

entries.farsi: يا چو بوى يوسف خوب لطيف / ‏مى‏زند بر جان يعقوب نحيف
entries.english: Or like the scent of beauteous, graceful Joseph, strikes upon the soul of lean Jacob.

entries.farsi: فايده‏ى ديگر كه هر خشتى كز اين / ‏بر كنم آيم سوى ماء معين
entries.english: The other advantage is that, every brick I tear off this, I come to running water,

entries.farsi: كز كمى خشت ديوار بلند / پست‏تر گردد به هر دفعه كه كند
entries.english: Since by removal of the bricks the high wall becomes lower every time that one is removed.

entries.farsi: پستى ديوار قربى مى‏شود / فصل او درمان وصلى مى‏بود
entries.english: The lowering of the wall becomes a means of access; separation from it is the remedy bringing about a union.”

entries.farsi: سجده آمد كندن خشت لزب / ‏موجب قربى كه وَ اسْ جُدْ وَ اقترب
entries.english: The tearing away of the adhesive bricks is prostration; the cause of nearness, for, ‘And prostrate thyself and draw near.

entries.farsi: تا كه اين ديوار عالى گردن است / ‏مانع اين سر فرود آوردن است
entries.english: So long as this wall is stiff-necked, it is an obstacle to this bowing of the head.

entries.farsi: بر سر ديوار هر كاو تشنه‏تر / زودتر بر مى‏كند خشت و مدر
entries.english: It is impossible to perform the prostration on the Water of Life, until I gain deliverance from this earthly body.

entries.farsi: سجده نتوان كرد بر آب حيات / ‏تا نيابم زين تن خاكى نجات
entries.english: The thirstier any one on the top of the wall is, the more quickly he tears off the bricks and turfs.

entries.farsi: هر كه عاشق تر بود بر بانگ آب / ‏او كلوخ زفت تر كند از حجاب
entries.english: The more any one is in love with the noise of the water, the bigger clods he tears away from the barrier.

entries.farsi: او ز بانگ آب پر مى تا عنق / ‏نشنود بيگانه جز بانگ بلق
entries.english: He, at the noise of the water, is filled with wine up to the neck, the stranger hears nothing but the sound of the splash.

entries.farsi: اى خنك آن را كه او ايام پيش / ‏مغتنم دارد گزارد وام خويش
entries.english: Oh, blest is he that deems his early days an opportunity to be seized, and pays his debt

entries.farsi: اندر آن ايام كش قدرت بود / صحت و زور دل و قوت بود
entries.english: In the days when he has the power, he has health and strength of heart and vigour,

entries.farsi: و آن جوانى همچو باغ سبز و تر / مى‏رساند بى‏دريغى بار و بر
entries.english: And that season of youth, like a garden green and fresh, is bringing produce and fruit without any stint;

entries.farsi: چشمه‏هاى قوت و شهوت روان / ‏سبز مى‏گردد زمين تن بدان
entries.english: The fountains of strength and lust flowing, thereby the soil of the body is made verdant;

entries.farsi: خانه‏ى معمور و سقفش بس بلند / معتدل اركان و بى‏تخليط و بند
entries.english: a well-kept house, with its roof very lofty, its sides symmetrical, without buttressing and clamps

entries.farsi: پيش از آن كه ايام پيرى در رسد / گردنت بندد به حَبْلٌ مِنْ مسد
entries.english: Before the days of old arrive and bind your neck with a halter of palm-fibres;

entries.farsi: خاك شوره گردد و ريزان و سست / هرگز از شوره نبات خوش نرست
entries.english: The soil becomes nitrous, crumbling, and poor — never did good herbage grow from nitrous soil;

entries.farsi: آب زور و آب شهوت منقطع / او ز خويش و ديگران نامنتفع
entries.english: The water of strength and the water of lust cut off, and he has no profit from himself or others:

entries.farsi: ابروان چون پالدم زير آمده / ‏چشم را نم آمده تارى شده
entries.english: The eyebrows fallen down like a crupper-strap; the eyes grown moist and dim;

entries.farsi: از تشنج رو چو پشت سوسمار / رفته نطق و طعم و دندانها ز كار
entries.english: The face, from wrinkling, like the back of a lizard; speech and taste and teeth gone out of use;

entries.farsi: روز بى‏گه الشه لنگ و ره دراز / كارگه ويران عمل رفته ز ساز
entries.english: The day late, the ass lame, and the way long; the shop ruined and the business in disorder;

entries.farsi: بيخهاى خوى بد محكم شده / ‏قوت بر كندن آن كم شده
entries.english: The roots of bad habit firmly set, and the power to tear them up decreased.

sections.title_fa: فرمودن والى آن مرد را كه اين خار بن را كه نشانده‏اى بر سر راه بر كن
sections.title_en: How the Governor commanded a certain man, saying, “Root up the thorn bush which you have planted on the road.”

entries.farsi: ره گذريانش مالمت‏گر شدند / بس بگفتندش بكن اين را نكند
entries.english: As that callous fair-spoken person planted a thorn bush in the middle of the road

entries.farsi: همچو آن شخص درشت خوش سخن / ‏در ميان ره نشاند او خار بن
entries.english: The wayfarers reproached him and often told him to dig it up: he did not dig it up.

entries.farsi: هر دمى آن خار بن افزون شدى / ‏پاى خلق از زخم آن پر خون شدى
entries.english: Every moment the thorn bush was growing bigger: the people’s feet were streaming with blood from its pricks.

entries.farsi: جامه‏هاى خلق بدريدى ز خار / پاى درويشان بخستى زار زار
entries.english: The people’s clothes were being torn by the thorns: the feet of the poor were being wounded pitiably.

entries.farsi: چون به جد حاكم بدو گفت اين بكن / ‏گفت آرى بر كنم روزيش من
entries.english: When the Governor said to him with earnestness, “Dig this up,” he replied, “Yes, I will dig it up some day”

entries.farsi: مدتى فردا و فردا وعده داد / شد درخت خار او محكم نهاد
entries.english: For a long while he promised to-morrow and tomorrow; his thorn bush became robust in constitution.

entries.farsi: گفت روزى حاكمش اى وعده كژ / پيش آ در كار ما واپس مغز
entries.english: One day the Governor said to him, “O false promiser, go forward with my affair, do not creep back.”

entries.farsi: گفت الايام يا عم بيننا / گفت عجل ال تماطل ديننا
entries.english: He replied, “O uncle, the days are between us.” “Make haste,” said he, “do not put off payment of my debt.”

entries.farsi: تو كه مى‏گويى كه فردا اين بدان / ‏كه به هر روزى كه مى‏آيد زمان
entries.english: You who say “To-morrow,” be aware of this, that with every day that time is coming,

entries.farsi: آن درخت بد جوان‏تر مى‏شود / وين كننده پير و مضطر مى‏شود
entries.english: That evil tree is growing younger, while this digger is waxing old and sorely distressed.

entries.farsi: خار بن در قوت و برخاستن / ‏خار كن در پيرى و در كاستن
entries.english: The thornbush in strength and ascent; its digger in aging and decline

entries.farsi: خار بن هر روز و هر دم سبز و تر / خار كن هر روز زار و خشكتر
entries.english: The thornbush every day and every moment is green and fresh; its digger is every day more sickly and withered.

entries.farsi: او جوانتر مى‏شود تو پيرتر / زود باش و روزگار خود مبر
entries.english: It is growing younger, you older: be quick and do not waste your time!

entries.farsi: خار بن دان هر يكى خوى بدت / ‏بارها در پاى خار آخر زدت
entries.english: Know that every single bad habit of yours is a thornbush: many a time, after all, have its thorns pierced your foot.

entries.farsi: بارها از خوى خود خسته شدى / ‏حس ندارى سخت بى‏حس آمدى
entries.english: Many a time have you been wounded by your own habits— you have no sense, you are very senseless.

entries.farsi: گر ز خسته گشتن ديگر كسان / ‏كه ز خلق زشت تو هست آن رسان
entries.english: If to the wounding of other persons, which comes to pass from your evil nature,

entries.farsi: غافلى بارى ز زخم خود نه‏اى / ‏تو عذاب خويش و هر بيگانه‏اى
entries.english: You are indifferent, at any rate you are not to your own wounds: you are the torment of yourself and of every stranger.

entries.farsi: يا تبر برگير و مردانه بزن / ‏تو على‏وار اين در خيبر بكن
entries.english: Either take up the axe and strike like a man— like ‘Ali, destroy this gate of Khaybar

entries.farsi: وصل كن با نار نور يار را / وصل او گلشن كند خار تو را
entries.english: Or unite these thorns with the rosebush: unite the light of the friend with the fire,

entries.farsi: يا به گلبن وصل كن اين خار را / وصل كن با نار نور يار را
entries.english: In order that his light may extinguish your fire, union with him may make your thorns roses.

entries.farsi: تو مثال دوزخى او مومن است / ‏كشتن آتش به مومن ممكن است
entries.english: You are like Hell, he is a true believer: the extinction of the fire by means of a true believer is possible.

entries.farsi: مصطفى فرمود از گفت جحيم / ‏كاو به مومن البه گر گردد ز بيم
entries.english: Mustafa said concerning the speech of Hell, that on account of fear it begins humbly to entreat the true believer,

entries.farsi: گويدش بگذر ز من اى شاه زود / هين كه نورت سوز نارم را ربود
entries.english: And says to him, “Pass quickly away from me, O king: hark, for thy light has taken away the burning of my fire.”

entries.farsi: پس هالك نار نور مومن است / ‏ز انكه بى‏ضد دفع ضد ال يمكن است
entries.english: Therefore the true believer’s light is the death of the fire, because without an opposite the removal of the opposite is impossible.

entries.farsi: نار ضد نور باشد روز عدل / ‏كان ز قهر انگيخته شد اين ز فضل
entries.english: On the Day of Justice the fire will be the opponent of the light, since the former was aroused by wrath, the latter by grace.

entries.farsi: گر همى‏خواهى تو دفع شر نار / آب رحمت بر دل آتش گمار
entries.english: If you are wishing to remove the evil of the fire, direct the water of mercy against the heart of the fire.

entries.farsi: چشمه‏ى آن آب رحمت مومن است / ‏آب حيوان روح پاك محسن است
entries.english: The true believer is the fountain of that water of mercy: the pure spirit of the well-doer is the Water of Life.

entries.farsi: بس گريزان است نفس تو از او / ز انكه تو از آتشى او آب جو
entries.english: Therefore your nafs is fleeing from him, because you are of fire, he the water of the stream.

entries.farsi: حس و فكر تو همه از آتش است / ‏حس شيخ و فكر او نور خوش است
entries.english: Fire becomes fleeing from water for the reason that its fire is ruined by water.

entries.farsi: ز آب آتش ز آن گريزان مى‏شود / كاتشش از آب ويران مى‏شود
entries.english: Your sense and thought are wholly of the fire; the sense of the Shaykh and his thought are the beauteous Light.

entries.farsi: آب نور او چو برآتش چكد / چك چك از آتش بر آيد بر جهد
entries.english: When the water of his light trickles on the fire, chak chak rises from the fire, and it leaps up

entries.farsi: چون كند چك چك تو گويش مرگ و درد / تا شود اين دوزخ نفس تو سرد
