sections.title_fa: تشنيع صوفيان بر آن صوفى كه پيش شيخ بسيار مى‏گويد
sections.title_en: How some Sufis abused a certain Sufi, saying that he talked too much in the presence of the Shaykh.

entries.farsi: شيخ را گفتند داد جان ما / تو از اين صوفى بجو اى پيشوا
entries.english: Some Sufis abused a certain Sufi, and came to the Shaykh of the convent,

entries.farsi: صوفيان بر صوفيى شنعت زدند / پيش شيخ خانقاهى آمدند
entries.english: And said to the Shaykh, “Demand justice for our souls from this Sufi, O Guide!”

entries.farsi: گفت آخر چه گله ست اى صوفيان / ‏گفت اين صوفى سه خو دارد گران
entries.english: He said, “Why, what is the complaint, O Sufis?” He replied, “This Sufi has three annoying habits:

entries.farsi: در سخن بسيار گو همچون جرس / در خورش افزون خورد از بيست كس
entries.english: In speech he is garrulous as a bell; in eating he eats more than twenty persons;

entries.farsi: ور بخسبد هست چون اصحاب كهف / ‏صوفيان كردند پيش شيخ زحف
entries.english: And if he sleeps, he is like the Men of the Cave.” did the Sufis march to war before the Shaykh.

entries.farsi: شيخ رو آورد سوى آن فقير / كه ز هر حالى كه هست اوساط گير
entries.english: The Shaykh turned his face towards that dervish, saying, “In every case that exists, take the middle.

entries.farsi: در خبر خير األمور أوساطها / نافع آمد ز اعتدال أخالطه
entries.english: In Tradition that the best things are the mean; the (four) humours are beneficial through being in balance.

entries.farsi: گر يكى خلطى فزون شد از عرض / ‏در تن مردم پديد آيد مرض
entries.english: If by accident one humour becomes excessive, disease appears in the human body.

entries.farsi: حالت من خواب را ماند گهى / ‏خواب پندارد مر آن را گمرهى
entries.english: At times my state resembles sleep: a misguided person may think it is sleep.

entries.farsi: چشم من خفته دلم بيدار دان / ‏شكل بى‏كار مرا بر كار دان
entries.english: Know that my eyes are asleep, my heart is awake: know that my inactive form is in action.

entries.farsi: گفت پيغمبر كه عيناى تنام / ‏ال ينام قلبي عن رب األنام
entries.english: The Prophet said, ‘My eyes sleep, my heart is not asleep to the Lord of created beings.’

entries.farsi: مر دلم را پنج حس ديگر است / ‏حس دل را هر دو عالم منظر است
entries.english: Your eyes are awake, and your heart is sunk in slumber; my eyes are asleep, my heart is in the opening of the door.

entries.farsi: چشم تو بيدار و دل خفته به خواب / ‏چشم من خفته دلم در فتح باب
entries.english: My heart has five senses other: both the worlds are the stage for the senses of the heart.

entries.farsi: تو ز ضعف خود مكن در من نگاه / ‏بر تو شب بر من همان شب چاشت‏گاه
entries.english: Do not regard me from your infirmity: to you it is night, to me that same night is morning tide.

entries.farsi: بر تو زندان بر من آن زندان چو باغ / ‏عين مشغولى مرا گشته فراغ
entries.english: To you it is prison, to me that prison is like a garden: to me the most absolute state of occupation has become freedom.

entries.farsi: در زمينم با تو ساكن در محل / ‏مى‏دوم بر چرخ هفتم چون زحل
entries.english: Your feet are in the mud; to me the mud has become roses. You have mourning; I have feasting and drums.

entries.farsi: پاى تو در گل مرا گل گشته گل / ‏مر ترا ماتم مرا سور و دهل
entries.english: I am dwelling with you in some place on the earth; I am coursing over the seventh sphere, like Saturn.

entries.farsi: همنشينت من نيم سايه‏ى من است / ‏برتر از انديشه‏ها پايه‏ى من است
entries.english: It is not I that am seated beside you, it is my shadow: my rank is higher than thoughts,

entries.farsi: ز انكه من ز انديشه‏ها بگذشته‏ام / ‏خارج انديشه پويان گشته‏ام
entries.english: Because I have passed beyond thoughts, and have become a swift traveller outside thought.

entries.farsi: حاكم انديشه‏ام محكوم نى / ز انكه بنا حاكم آمد بر بنا
entries.english: I am the ruler of thought, not ruled, because the builder is ruler over the building.

entries.farsi: جمله خلقان سخره‏ى انديشه‏اند / ز آن سبب خسته دل و غم پيشه‏اند
entries.english: All creatures are subjugated to thought; for that reason they are sore in heart and practised in sorrow.

entries.farsi: قاصدا خود را به انديشه دهم / ‏چون بخواهم از ميانشان بر جهم
entries.english: I yield myself to thought purposely; when I will I spring up from the midst of them.

entries.farsi: من چو مرغ اوجم انديشه مگس / ‏كى بود بر من مگس را دست‏رس
entries.english: I am as a bird of the zenith, thought is a gnat: how should a gnat have power over me?

entries.farsi: ‏كى بود بر من مگس را دست‏رس / تا شكسته پايگان بر من تنند
entries.english: Purposely I come down from the lofty zenith that those of base degree may attain to me.

entries.farsi: چون ماللم گيرد از سفلى صفات / ‏بر پرم همچون طيور الصافات
entries.english: When disgust at the qualities of the low seizes me, I soar up like the birds which spread their pinions.

entries.farsi: پر من رسته ست هم از ذات خويش / ‏بر نچسبانم دو پر من با سريش
entries.english: My wings have grown out of my very essence: I do not stick two wings on with glue.

entries.farsi: جعفر طيار را پر جاريه ست / ‏جعفر عيار را پر عاريه ست
entries.english: The wings of Jafar-i Tayyar are permanent; the wings of Jafar-i ‘Ayyar are borrowed.

entries.farsi: نزد آن كه لم يذق دعوى است اين / ‏نزد سكان افق معنى است اين
entries.english: In the view of him that has not experienced, this is pretension; in the view of the inhabitants of the horizon, this is the reality.

entries.farsi: الف و دعوى باشد اين پيش غراب / ‏ديگ تى و پر يكى پيش ذباب
entries.english: This is brag and pretension in the eyes of the crow: an empty or full pot is all one to the fly.

entries.farsi: چون كه در تو مى‏شود لقمه گهر / تن مزن چندان كه بتوانى بخور
entries.english: When morsels of food become pearls within you, do not delay: eat as much as you can.”

entries.farsi: شيخ روزى بهر دفع سوء ظن / ‏در لگن قى كرد پر در شد لگن
entries.english: One day the Shaykh, in order to rebut ill thoughts, vomited in a basin, and the basin became full of pearls.

entries.farsi: گوهر معقول را محسوس كرد / پير بينا بهر كم عقلى مرد
entries.english: On account of the man’s little understanding, the clairvoyant Pir made the intelligible pearls objects of sense-perception.

entries.farsi: چون كه در معده شود پاكت پليد / قفل نه بر حلق و پنهان كن كليد
entries.english: When pure turns to impurity in your stomach, put a lock upon your gullet and hide the key;

entries.farsi: هر كه در وى لقمه شد نور جلال / ‏هر چه خواهد تا خورد او را حالل
entries.english: Any one in whom morsels of food become the light of glory, let him eat whatever he will, it is lawful to him.

sections.title_fa: ‏بيان دعويى كه عين آن دعوى گواه صدق خويش است
sections.title_en: Explaining some assertions the truth of which is attested by their very nature

entries.farsi: گر تو هستى آشناى جان من / ‏نيست دعوى گفت معنى الن من
entries.english: If you are my soul’s familiar friend, my words full of meaning are not assertion.

entries.farsi: گر بگويم نيم شب پيش توام / ‏هين مترس از شب كه من خويش توام
entries.english: If at midnight I say, “I am near you: come now, be not afraid of the night, for I am your kinsman,”

entries.farsi: اين دو دعوى پيش تو معنى بود / چون شناسى بانگ خويشاوند خود
entries.english: These two assertions are to you reality, since you recognise the voice of your own relative.

entries.farsi: پيشى و خويشى دو دعوى بود ليك / ‏هر دو معنى بود پيش فهم نيك
entries.english: Nearness and kinship were two assertions, but both were reality to the good understanding.

entries.farsi: قرب آوازش گواهى مى‏دهد / كاين دم از نزديك يارى مى‏جهد
entries.english: The proximity of the voice gives him testimony that these words spring from a friend;

entries.farsi: لذت آواز خويشاوند نيز / شد گوا بر صدق آن خويش عزيز
entries.english: Moreover, delight at the voice of his kinsman has borne witness to the truthfulness of that dear relative.

entries.farsi: باز بى‏الهام احمق كاو ز جهل / ‏مى‏نداند بانگ بيگانه ز اهل
entries.english: Again, the uninspired fool who in his ignorance does not know a stranger’s voice from a kinsman’s

entries.farsi: پيش او دعوى بود گفتار او / جهل او شد مايه‏ى انكار او
entries.english: To him his words are assertion: his ignorance has become the source of his disbelief;

entries.farsi: پيش زيرك كاندرونش نورهاست / ‏عين اين آواز معنى بود راست
entries.english: To him of keen insight, within whom are the lights, the very nature of this voice was just the reality.

entries.farsi: عين تازى گفتنش معنى بود / گر چه تازى گفتنش دعوى بود
entries.english: Or one whose mother-tongue is Arabic says in Arabic, “I know the language of the Arabs.”

entries.farsi: يا به تازى گفت يك تازى زبان / ‏كه همى‏دانم زبان تازيان
entries.english: The very fact of his speaking in Arabic is the reality, although his saying Arabic is an assertion.

entries.farsi: يا نويسد كاتبى بر كاغذى / ‏كاتب و خط خوانم و من ابجدى
entries.english: Or a writer may write on a piece of paper, “I am a writer and a reader, and I am a most accomplished person.”

entries.farsi: اين نوشته گر چه خود دعوى بود / هم نوشته شاهد معنى بود
entries.english: Although this writing itself is an assertion, still the script is evidence of the reality.

entries.farsi: يا بگويد صوفيى ديدى تو دوش / ‏در ميان خواب سجاده به دوش
entries.english: Or a Sufi may say, “Last night, while asleep, you saw some one with a prayer-carpet on his shoulder.

entries.farsi: من بدم آن و آن چه گفتم خواب در / با تو اندر خواب در شرح نظر
entries.english: That was I; and what I said to you in the dream, whilst you slumbered, in explanation of clairvoyance

entries.farsi: گوش كن چون حلقه اندر گوش كن / ‏آن سخن را پيشواى هوش كن
entries.english: Give ear, put it in your ear like an ear-ring: make those words your mind’s guide.”

entries.farsi: چون ترا ياد آيد آن خواب اين سخن / ‏معجز نو باشد و زر كهن
entries.english: When you recollect the dream, these words are a new miracle or old gold.

entries.farsi: گر چه دعوى مى‏نمايد اين ولى / ‏جان صاحب واقعه گويد بلى
entries.english: Although this seems to be assertion, yet the soul of the dreamer says, “Yes,.”

entries.farsi: پس چو حكمت ضاله‏ى مومن بود / آن ز هر كه بشنود موقن بود
entries.english: Therefore, since Wisdom is the faithful believer’s stray camel, he knows it with certainty; from whomsoever he has heard it;

entries.farsi: چون كه خود را پيش او يابد فقط / چون بود شك چون كند او را غلط
entries.english: And when he finds himself absolutely in front of it, how should there be doubt? How should he mistake himself?

entries.farsi: تشنه‏اى را چون بگويى تو شتاب / ‏در قدح آب است بستان زود آب
entries.english: When you say to a thirsty man, “Make haste! There is water in the cup: take the water at once,”

entries.farsi: هيچ گويد تشنه كاين دعوى است رو / از برم اى مدعى مهجور شو
entries.english: Will the thirsty man say in any event?— “This is assertion: go from my side, O pretender! Get you far away!

entries.farsi: يا گواه و حجتى بنما كه اين / ‏جنس آب است و از آن ماء معين
entries.english: Or produce some testimony and proof that this is of aqueous kind and consists of the water that runs from a spring.

entries.farsi: يا به طفل شير مادر بانگ زد / كه بيا من مادرم هان اى ولد
entries.english: Or a mother cries to her suckling babe, “Come, I am mother: listen, my child!”—

entries.farsi: طفل گويد مادرا حجت بيار / تا كه با شيرت بگيرم من قرار
entries.english: Will the babe say?—” O mother, bring the proof, so that I may take comfort in your milk.”

entries.farsi: در دل هر امتى كز حق مزه ست / ‏روى و آواز پيمبر معجزه ست
entries.english: When in the heart of any community there is savour from God, the face and voice of the prophet are an evidentiary miracle.

entries.farsi: چون پيمبر از برون بانگى زند / جان امت در درون سجده كند
entries.english: When the prophet utters a cry from without, the soul of the community falls to worship within,

entries.farsi: ز انكه جنس بانگ او اندر جهان / ‏از كسى نشنيده باشد گوش جان
entries.english: Because never in the world will the soul’s ear have heard from any one a cry of the same kind as his.

entries.farsi: آن غريب از ذوق آواز غريب / ‏از زبان حق شنود انى قريب
entries.english: That stranger, by immediate perception of the strange voice, has heard from God’s tongue, “Truly I am near.”

sections.title_fa: ‏سجده كردن يحيى عليه السلام در شكم مادر مسيح را عليه السلام
sections.title_en: How Yahya, on whom be peace, in his mother’s womb bowed in worship to the Messiah, on whom be peace.

entries.farsi: كه يقين ديدم درون تو شهى است / ‏كاو اولو العزم و رسول آگهى است
entries.english: The mother of Yahya, before disburdening herself, said in secret to Mary,

entries.farsi: مادر يحيى به مريم در نهفت / ‏پيشتر از وضع حمل خويش گفت
entries.english: “I see with certainty, within you is a King who is possessed of firm purpose and is an Apostle endowed with knowledge.

entries.farsi: چون برابر اوفتادم با تو من / ‏كرد سجده حمل من اندر زمن
entries.english: When I happened to meet you, my burden at once bowed in worship.

entries.farsi: اين جنين مر آن جنين را سجده كرد / كز سجودش در تنم افتاد درد
entries.english: This embryo bowed in worship to that embryo, so that pain arose in my body from its bowing.”

entries.farsi: گفت مريم من درون خويش هم / ‏سجده‏اى ديدم از اين طفل شكم
entries.english: Mary said, “ I also felt within me a bowing performed by this babe in the womb.”

sections.title_fa: اشكال آوردن بر اين قصه
sections.title_en: On raising a difficulty as to this story

entries.farsi: ابلهان گويند كاين افسانه را / خط بكش زيرا دروغ است و خطا
entries.english: The foolish say, “Cancel this tale, because it is false and erroneous.

entries.farsi: ز انكه مريم وقت وضع حمل خويش / ‏بود از بيگانه دور و هم ز خويش
entries.english: Mary in pregnancy was not joined by any one: she did not return from without the town.

entries.farsi: از برون شهر آن شيرين فسون / ‏تا نشد فارغ نيامد خود درون
entries.english: Until that woman of sweet address was delivered outside of the town, she indeed came not into it.

entries.farsi: چون بزادش آن گهانش بر كنار / بر گرفت و برد تا پيش تبار
entries.english: When she had given birth to him, she then took him up in her lap and carried him to her kinsfolk.

entries.farsi: مادر يحيى كجا ديدش كه تا / گويد او را اين سخن در ماجرا
entries.english: Where did the mother of Yahya see her to speak these words to her about what had happened?”

sections.title_fa: ‏جواب اشكال

entries.farsi: اين بداند كان كه اهل خاطر است / ‏غايب آفاق او را حاضر است
entries.english: The answer to the difficulty

entries.farsi: پيش مريم حاضر آيد در نظر / مادر يحيى كه دور است از بصر
entries.english: Let him know that to one who receives ideas all that is absent in the world is present.

entries.farsi: ديده‏ها بسته ببيند دوست را / چون مشبك كرده باشد پوست را
entries.english: To Mary, the mother of Yahya would appear present, though she was far from her sight.

entries.farsi: ور نديدش نه از برون نز اندرون / ‏از حكايت گير معنى اى زبون
entries.english: One may see a friend with eyes shut, when one has made the skin a lattice.

entries.farsi: نه چنان كافسانه‏ها بشنيده بود / همچو شين بر نقش آن چسبيده بود
entries.english: And if she saw her neither from without nor from within, take the meaning of the story, O imbecile!

entries.farsi: تا همى‏گفت آن كليله بى‏زبان / ‏چون سخن نوشد ز دمنه بى‏بيان
entries.english: Not like him who had heard fables, and like sh stuck to the shape of them,

entries.farsi: در ميان شير و گاو آن دمنه چون / ‏شد رسول و خواند بر هر دو فسون
entries.english: So that he would say, “How should Kalila, having no language, hear words from Dimna who had no power of expression?

entries.farsi: ور بدانستند لحن همدگر / فهم آن چون كرد بى‏نطقى بشر
entries.english: And if they knew each other’s accents, how should man understand it, without any articulation?

entries.farsi: چون وزير شير شد گاو نبيل / ‏چون ز عكس ماه ترسان گشت پيل
entries.english: How did Dimna become a messenger between the lion and the ox, and cajole them both with his palaver?

entries.farsi: اين كليله و دمنه جمله افترى است / ‏ور نه كى با زاغ لكلك را مرى است
entries.english: How did the noble ox become the vizier of the lion? How was the elephant terrified by the reflection of the moon?

entries.farsi: اى برادر قصه چون پيمانه‏اى است / ‏معنى اندر وى مثال دانه‏اى است
entries.english: This Kalila and Dimna is entirely fiction, or else how has the stork a quarrel with the crow?”

entries.farsi: دانه‏ى معنى بگيرد مرد عقل / ‏ننگرد پيمانه را گر گشت نقل
entries.english: O brother, the story is like a measure: the real meaning in it resembles grain.

entries.farsi: ماجراى بلبل و گل گوش دار / گر چه گفتى نيست آن جا آشكار
entries.english: The man of intelligence will take the grain of meaning: he will not pay any regard to the measure, if it is removed.
Listen to what passes between the rose and the nightingale, though in that case there is no overt speech.

sections.title_fa: سخن گفتن به زبان حال و فهم كردن آن
sections.title_en: On mute eloquence and the understanding of it

entries.farsi: ماجراى شمع با پروانه نيز / بشنو و معنى گزين كن اى عزيز
entries.english: Listen also to what passes between the moth and the candle, and pick out the meaning, O worshipful one.

entries.farsi: گر چه گفتى نيست سر گفت هست / ‏هين ببالا پر مپر چون جغد پست
entries.english: Although there is no speech, there is the inmost soul of speech. Come, fly aloft, do not fly low, like the owl.

entries.farsi: گفت در شطرنج كاين خانه‏ى رخ است / ‏گفت خانه از كجاش آمد بدست
entries.english: He at chess said, “This is the house of the rook.” “By what way,” said he, did the house come into its hands?

entries.farsi: خانه را بخريد يا ميراث يافت / ‏فرخ آن كس كاو سوى معنى شتافت
entries.english: Did it buy the house, or inherit it? “— happy is he that sped towards the meaning!

entries.farsi: گفت نحوى زيد عمرا قد ضرب / ‏گفت چونش كرد بى‏جرمى ادب
entries.english: A grammarian said, “Zayd has struck `Amr.” Said (the fool), “How did he chastise him without any offence?

entries.farsi: عمرو را جرمش چه بد كان زيد خام / ‏بى‏گنه او را بزد همچون غالم
entries.english: What was ‘Amr’s offence, that that rude Zayd struck him, innocent, as a slave?”

entries.farsi: گفت اين پيمانه‏ى معنى بود / گندمى بستان كه پيمانه است رد
entries.english: He replied, “This is the measure of the meaning signified: take some wheat, for the measure is rejected.

entries.farsi: زيد و عمرو از بهر اعراب است و ساز / گر دروغ است آن تو با اعراب ساز
entries.english: Zayd and `Amr are a device for the declension: if that is untrue, make up with the declension.”

entries.farsi: گفت نه من آن ندانم عمرو را / زيد چون زد بى‏گناه و بى‏خطا
entries.english: “No,” said he, “I don’t know about that. How did Zayd strike `Amr without crime or fault?”

entries.farsi: گفت از ناچار و الغى بر گشود / عمرو يك واو فزون دزديده بود
entries.english: He in desperation started a joke and said, “ `Amr had stolen a superfluous waw.

entries.farsi: زيد واقف گشت دزدش را بزد / چون كه از حد برد او را حد سزد
entries.english: Zayd became aware, and struck the one who stole it: since he carried it beyond bounds, the punishment serves him right.”

sections.title_fa: ‏پذيرا آمدن سخن باطل در دل باطالن
sections.title_en: How worthless sayings find acceptance in the minds of worthless folk.

entries.farsi: گر بگويى احولى را مه يكى است / ‏گويدت اين دوست و در وحدت شكى است
entries.english: He said, “Here you are, this is the truth! I accept with my soul.” Wrong seems right to the wrong-minded.

entries.farsi: گفت اينك راست پذرفتم به جان / ‏كج نمايد راست در پيش كجان
entries.english: If you say to a squinting man, “The moon is one,” he will say to you, “These are two; and there is a doubt as to being one”;

entries.farsi: ور بر او خندد كسى گويد دو است / ‏راست دارد اين سزاى بد خو است
entries.english: And if somebody laughs at him and says, “There are two,” he deems it the truth. This is what the ill-natured fellow deserves.

entries.farsi: بر دروغان جمع مى‏آيد دروغ / ‏الخبيثات الخبيثين زد فروغ
entries.english: Lies muster round lies: the phrase wicked women for the wicked men has thrown light.

entries.farsi: دل فراخان را بود دست فراخ / ‏چشم كوران را عثار سنگ‏الخ
entries.english: They whose hearts are wide have wide hands; they whose eyes are blind have to stumble on stony ground.

sections.title_fa: جستن آن درخت كه هر كه ميوه‏ى آن درخت خورد نميرد
sections.title_en: On seeking the tree whereof none that eats the fruit shall die.

entries.farsi: گفت دانايى براى داستان / ‏كه درختى هست در هندوستان
entries.english: A learned man said, for the sake of a story, “In India there is a certain tree:

entries.farsi: هر كسى كز ميوه‏ى او خورد و برد / نه شود او پير نه هرگز بمرد
entries.english: Whoso takes and eats of its fruit, he grows not old nor ever dies.”

entries.farsi: پادشاهى اين شنيد از صادقى / ‏بر درخت و ميوه‏اش شد عاشقى
entries.english: A king heard this from a veracious person: he became a lover of the tree and its fruit,

entries.farsi: قاصدى دانا ز ديوان ادب / ‏سوى هندستان روان كرد از طلب
entries.english: From the Divan of culture he sent an intelligent envoy to India in search.

entries.farsi: سالها مى‏گشت آن قاصد از او / گرد هندستان براى جستجو
entries.english: For years his envoy wandered about India in quest.

entries.farsi: شهر شهر از بهر اين مطلوب گشت / ‏نه جزيره ماند و نه كوه و نه دشت
entries.english: He roamed from town to town for this object: neither island nor mountain nor plain was left.

entries.farsi: هر كه را پرسيد كردش ريشخند / كاين كه جويد جز مگر مجنون بند
entries.english: Every one whom he asked made a mock of him, saying, “Who would search after this, unless perhaps a madman in confinement?”

entries.farsi: بس كسان صفعش زدند اندر مزاح / ‏بس كسان گفتند اى صاحب فالح
entries.english: Many slapped him jokingly; many said, “O fortunate man,

entries.farsi: جستجوى چون تو زيرك سينه صاف / ‏كى تهى باشد كجا باشد گزاف
entries.english: How should the enquiry of a clever and clear-minded person like you be devoid? How should it be vain?”

entries.farsi: وين مراعاتش يكى صفعى دگر / وين ز صفع آشكارا سخت‏تر
entries.english: And this respect was to him another slap, and it was harder than the visible slap.

entries.farsi: مى‏ستودندش به تسخر كاى بزرگ / ‏در فالن اقليم بس هول و سترگ
entries.english: They extolled him sarcastically, saying, “O great sir, in such and such a place there is a very huge tree.

entries.farsi: در فالن بيشه درختى هست سبز / بس بلند و پهن و هر شاخيش گبز
entries.english: In such and such a forest there is a green tree, very tall and broad, and every branch of it is big.”

entries.farsi: قاصد شه بسته در جستن كمر / مى‏شنيد از هر كسى نوعى خبر
entries.english: The king’s envoy, who had braced his belt for the quest, was hearing a different kind of report from every one;

entries.farsi: بس سياحت كرد آن جا سالها / مى‏فرستادش شهنشه مالها
entries.english: So he travelled there for years, the king kept sending money to him.

entries.farsi: چون بسى ديد اندر آن غربت تعب / ‏عاجز آمد آخر الامر از طلب
entries.english: After he had suffered much fatigue in that foreign land, at last he became too exhausted to seek.

entries.farsi: هيچ از مقصود اثر پيدا نشد / ز آن غرض غير خبر پيدا نشد
entries.english: No trace of the object of pursuit was discovered: of what he wanted nothing appeared but the report.

entries.farsi: رشته‏ى اميد او بگسسته شد / جسته‏ى او عاقبت ناجسته شد
entries.english: The thread of his hope snapped, the thing he had sought became unsought in the end.

entries.farsi: كرد عزم باز گشتن سوى شاه / ‏اشك مى‏باريد و مى‏بريد راه
entries.english: He resolved to return to the king, shedding tears and traversing the way.

sections.title_fa: شرح كردن شيخ سر آن درخت را با آن طالب مقلد
sections.title_en: How the Shaykh explained the hidden meaning of the tree to the seeker who was in the bondage of formalism.

entries.farsi: بود شيخى عالمى قطبى كريم / ‏اندر آن منزل كه آيس شد نديم
entries.english: There was a wise Shaykh, a noble Qutb, at the halting-place where the king’s intimate fell into despair.

entries.farsi: گفت من نوميد پيش او روم / ‏ز آستان او به راه اندر شوم
entries.english: He said, “ Being without hope, I will go to him, and set out on the road from his threshold,

entries.farsi: تا دعاى او بود همراه من / ‏چون كه نوميدم من از دل خواه من
entries.english: In order that his prayer may accompany me, since I have no hope of my heart’s desire.”

entries.farsi: رفت پيش شيخ با چشم پر آب / ‏اشك مى‏باريد مانند سحاب
entries.english: With tearful eyes he went to the Shaykh: he was raining tears, like a cloud.

entries.farsi: گفت شيخا وقت رحم و رقت است / ‏نااميدم وقت لطف اين ساعت است
entries.english: “O Shaykh,” he cried,” it is the time for mercy and pity; I am in despair: now is the time for kindness.”

entries.farsi: گفت وا گو كز چه نوميديستت / ‏چيست مطلوب تو رو با چيستت
entries.english: He said, “ Say plainly what the cause of your despair is: what is your object? What have you in view?”

entries.farsi: گفت شاهنشاه كردم اختيار / از براى جستن يك شاخسار
entries.english: He answered, “The Emperor chose me out to seek a certain branching tree,

entries.farsi: كه درختى هست نادر در جهات / ‏ميوه‏ى او مايه‏ى آب حيات
entries.english: For there is a tree, unique in the quarters: its fruit is the substance of the Water of Life

entries.farsi: سالها جستم نديدم يك نشان / ‏جز كه طنز و تسخر اين سر خوشان
entries.english: I have sought for years and seen no sign except the gibes and ridicule of these merry men.”

entries.farsi: شيخ خنديد و بگفتش اى سليم / ‏اين درخت علم باشد در عليم
entries.english: The Shaykh laughed and said to him, “O simpleton, this is the tree of knowledge in the sage

entries.farsi: بس بلند و بس شگرف و بس بسيط / آب حيوانى ز درياى محيط
entries.english: Very high and very grand and very far-spreading: a Water of Life from the all-encompassing Sea.

entries.farsi: تو به صورت رفته‏اى اى بى‏خبر / ز آن ز شاخ معنيى بى‏بار و بر
entries.english: You have gone after the form, you have gone astray: you can not find because you have abandoned the reality

entries.farsi: گه درختش نام شد گه آفتاب / ‏گاه بحرش نام گشت و گه سحاب
entries.english: Sometimes it is named ‘tree,’ sometimes ‘sun’; sometimes it is named ‘sea,’ sometimes ‘cloud.’

entries.farsi: آن يكى كش صد هزار آثار خاست / ‏كمترين آثار او عمر بقاست
entries.english: That one from which a hundred thousand effects arise: its least effects are everlasting life.

entries.farsi: گر چه فرد است او اثر دارد هزار / اين يكى را نام شايد بى‏شمار
entries.english: Although it is single, it bath a thousand effects: innumerable names befit that one.

entries.farsi: آن يكى شخص ترا باشد پدر / در حق شخصى دگر باشد پسر
entries.english: One person may be father in relation to you; in regard to another individual he may be son.

entries.farsi: در حق ديگر بود قهر و عدو / در حق ديگر بود لطف و نكو
entries.english: In regard to another he may be wrath and a foe; in regard to another he may be graciousness and a friend.

entries.farsi: صد هزاران نام و او يك آدمى / ‏صاحب هر وصفش از وصفى عمى
entries.english: Hundreds of thousands of names, he is one man: the owner of every quality belonging to him is blind to giving any description.

entries.farsi: هر كه جويد نام اگر صاحب ثقه است / ‏همچو تو نوميد و اندر تفرقه است
entries.english: Whoever seeks the name, if he is entrusted he is hopeless and in distraction, even as you art.

entries.farsi: تو چه بر چفسى بر اين نام درخت / ‏تا بمانى تلخ كام و شور بخت
entries.english: Why dost you stick to the name ‘tree,’ so that you art left bitterly disappointed and ill-fortuned?

entries.farsi: در گذر از نام و بنگر در صفات / ‏تا صفاتت ره نمايد سوى ذات
entries.english: Pass on from the name and look at the attributes, in order that the attributes may show you the way to the essence.”

entries.farsi: اختالف خلق از نام اوفتاد / چون به معنى رفت آرام اوفتاد
entries.english: The disagreement of mankind is caused by names: peace ensues when they advance to the reality.

sections.title_fa: منازعت چهار كس جهت انگور كه هر يكى به نام ديگر فهم كرده بود آن را
sections.title_en: How four persons quarrelled about grapes, which were known to each of them by a different name

entries.farsi: چار كس را داد مردى يك درم / ‏آن يكى گفت اين به انگورى دهم
entries.english: A certain man gave a dirhem to four persons: one of them said, “I will spend this on angur.”

entries.farsi: آن يكى ديگر عرب بد گفت ال / من عنب خواهم نه انگور اى دغا
entries.english: The second one was an Arab: he said, “No, I want ‘inab, not angur, O rascal!”

entries.farsi: آن يكى تركى بدو گفت اى گزم / ‏من نمى‏خواهم عنب خواهم ازم
entries.english: The third was a Turk; and he said, “This is mine: I don’t want `inab, I want uzum.”

entries.farsi: آن يكى رومى بگفت اين قيل را / ترك كن خواهيم استافيل را
entries.english: The fourth, a Greek, said, “Stop this talk: I want istafil.”

entries.farsi: در تنازع آن نفر جنگى شدند / كه ز سر نامها غافل بدند
entries.english: These people began fighting in contention with one another, because they were unaware of the hidden meaning of the names

entries.farsi: مشت بر هم مى‏زدند از ابلهى / ‏پر بدند از جهل و از دانش تهى
entries.english: In their folly they smote each other with their fists: they were full of ignorance and empty of knowledge

entries.farsi: صاحب سرى عزيزى صد زبان / ‏گر بدى آن جا بدادى صلح‏شان
entries.english: If a master of the esoteric had been there, a revered and much languaged man, he would have pacified them;

entries.farsi: پس بگفتى او كه من زين يك درم / ‏آرزوى جمله‏تان را مى‏خرم
entries.english: And then he would have said, “With this one dirhem I will give all of you what you wish.

entries.farsi: چون كه بسپاريد دل را بى‏دغل / ‏اين درمتان مى‏كند چندين عمل
entries.english: When without deceit you surrender your hearts, this dirhem will do all this for you.

entries.farsi: يك درمتان مى‏شود چار المراد / چار دشمن مى‏شود يك ز اتحاد
entries.english: Your one dirhem will become four—the result desired: four enemies will become one through unanimity.

entries.farsi: پس شما خاموش باشيد أنصتوا / تا زبان تان من شوم در گفت‏وگو
entries.english: What each one of you says produces strife and separation; what I say brings you agreement.

entries.farsi: گفت هر يك تان دهد جنگ و فراق / ‏گفت من آرد شما را اتفاق
entries.english: Therefore be you mute, keep silence, that I may be your tongue in speech and talk.”

entries.farsi: گر سخنتان مى‏نمايد يك نمط / در اثر مايه‏ى نزاع است و سخط
entries.english: If in their agreement with each other your words are a strong rope, in effect they are a source of contention and distraction.

entries.farsi: گرمى عاريتى ندهد اثر / گرمى خاصيتى دارد هنر
entries.english: Borrowed heat produces no effect; natural heat has virtue.

entries.farsi: سركه را گر گرم كردى ز آتش آن / ‏چون خورى سردى فزايد بى‏گمان
entries.english: If you have made vinegar hot by means of fire, when you drink it, it will undoubtedly increase the coldness,

entries.farsi: ز انكه آن گرمى او دهليزى است / ‏طبع اصلش سردى است و تيزى است
entries.english: Because that heat of it is exotic: its fundamental nature is coldness and tartness.

entries.farsi: ور بود يخ بسته دوشاب اى پسر / چون خورى گرمى فزايد در جگر
entries.english: And, though grape-syrup be frozen, my son, it will add heat to the liver when you drink it.

entries.farsi: پس رياى شيخ به ز اخالص ماست / ‏كز بصيرت باشد آن وين از عماست
entries.english: Hence the Shaykh’s hypocrisy is better than our sincerity, for the former arises from insight, while the latter arises from blindness.

entries.farsi: از حديث شيخ جمعيت رسد / تفرقه آرد دم اهل حسد
entries.english: From the Shaykh’s discourse comes union; the words of the materialists bring separation.

entries.farsi: چون سليمان كز سوى حضرت بتاخت / ‏كاو زبان جمله مرغان را شناخت
entries.english: As Solomon, who sped from God, and who knew the language of all birds

entries.farsi: در زمان عدلش آهو با پلنگ / ‏انس بگرفت و برون آمد ز جنگ
entries.english: In the time of his just sway the deer made friends with the leopard and ceased from war.

entries.farsi: شد كبوتر ايمن از چنگال باز / گوسفند از گرگ ناورد احتراز
entries.english: The dove became secure from the talons of the hawk; the sheep took no precaution against the wolf.

entries.farsi: او ميانجى شد ميان دشمنان / ‏اتحادى شد ميان پر زنان
entries.english: He became an arbitrator between enemies: he became an oneness between the creatures that fly with wings.

entries.farsi: تو چو مورى بهر دانه مى‏دوى / ‏هين سليمان جو چه مى‏باشى غوى
entries.english: You are running after grain, like an ant. Listen! Seek Solomon! Why are you still astray?

entries.farsi: دانه جو را دانه‏اش دامى شود / و آن سليمان جوى را هر دو بود
entries.english: To the seeker of grain his grain becomes a snare, but the seeker of Solomon may have both.

entries.farsi: مرغ جانها را در اين آخر زمان / ‏نيستشان از همدگر يك دم امان
entries.english: In these latter days the soul-birds have no security from each other for a moment;

entries.farsi: هم سليمان هست اندر دور ما / كاو دهد صلح و نماند جور ما
entries.english: Even in our epoch there is a Solomon who would give peace and not suffer our injustice to continue.

entries.farsi: قول إِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ را ياد گير / تا به إال و خَال فِيها نذير
entries.english: Call to mind, There is no people down to but in the past a warner dwelt among them.

entries.farsi: گفت خود خالى نبوده ست امتى / ‏از خليفه‏ى حق و صاحب همتى
entries.english: God said that verily there have never been a people devoid of a vicar of God and a man of spiritual power;

entries.farsi: مرغ جانها را چنان يكدل كند / كز صفاشان بى‏غش و بى‏غل كند
entries.english: And he makes the soul-birds so unanimous that, in respect of sincerity, he purges them of guile and rancour.

entries.farsi: مشفقان گردند همچون والده / ‏مسلمون را گفت نفس واحده
entries.english: They become kind as a mother: he said of the Moslems, “One soul.”

entries.farsi: نفس واحد از رسول حق شدند / ور نه هر يك دشمن مطلق بدند
entries.english: Through the Messenger of Allah they became one soul; else, they were absolute enemies, every one.

sections.title_fa: ‏برخاستن مخالفت و عداوت از ميان انصار به بركات رسول صلى اللهَّ عليه و آله
sections.title_en: How dissension and enmity amongst the Ansar were removed by the blessings of the Prophet—may God bless and save him!

entries.farsi: دو قبيله كاوس و خزرج نام داشت / ‏يك ز ديگر جان خون آشام داشت
entries.english: The two tribes which were named Aws and Khazraj had a blood-thirsty spirit towards each other.

entries.farsi: كينه‏هاى كهنه‏شان از مصطفى / محو شد در نور اسالم و صفا
entries.english: Through Mustafa their ancient feuds vanished in the light of Islam and of pureness.

entries.farsi: اوال اخوان شدند آن دشمنان / ‏همچو اعداد عنب در بوستان
entries.english: First, those enemies became brethren like the units of grapes in the garden;

entries.farsi: و ز دم الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْ وَةٌ به پند / در شكستند و تن واحد شدند
entries.english: And at the admonition given in the words, The true believers are brethren, they dissolved and became one body.

entries.farsi: صورت انگورها اخوان بود / چون فشردى شيره‏ى واحد شود
entries.english: The appearance of the grapes is brethren: when you squeeze them they become one juice.

entries.farsi: غوره و انگور ضدانند ليك / ‏چون كه غوره پخته شد شد يار نيك
entries.english: The immature and the full-grown grape are opponents, but when the immature grape has ripened, it becomes a good friend.

entries.farsi: غوره‏اى كاو سنگ بست و خام ماند / در ازل حق كافر اصليش خواند
entries.english: The immature grape that has remained stone-hard and crude — God in eternity has called it an original unbeliever.

entries.farsi: نه اخى نه نفس واحد باشد او / در شقاوت نحس ملحد باشد او
entries.english: He is neither a brother nor one soul: he is an ill-starred heretic in damnation.

entries.farsi: گر بگويم آن چه او دارد نهان / ‏فتنه‏ى افهام خيزد در جهان
entries.english: If I should tell that which he keeps hidden, there would arise in the world a sore temptation of minds.

entries.farsi: سر گبر كور نامذكور به / ‏دود دوزخ از ارم مهجور به
entries.english: It is better that the secret of the blind infidel should be untold: it is better that the smoke of Hell should be banished from Iram.

entries.farsi: سوى انگورى همى‏رانند تيز / تا دويى برخيزد و كين و ستيز
entries.english: The good immature grapes, which are capable, are at last one in heart by the breath of the masters of heart.

entries.farsi: غوره‏هاى نيك كايشان قابل‏اند / از دم اهل دل آخر يك دل‏اند
entries.english: They push on rapidly to grapehood, so that duality and hatred and strife depart.

entries.farsi: پس در انگورى همى‏درند پوست / ‏تا يكى گردند و وحدت وصف اوست
entries.english: Then in grapehood they rend their skins till they become one: unity is the attribute of him.

entries.farsi: دوست دشمن گردد ايرا هم دو است / ‏هيچ يك با خويش جنگى در نبست
entries.english: A friend becomes a foe because he is still two: is any one in a quarrel with himself?

entries.farsi: آفرين بر عشق كل اوستاد / صد هزاران ذره را داد اتحاد
entries.english: Blessings on the universal love of the Master, gave oneness to hundreds of thousands of motes!

entries.farsi: همچو خاك مفترق در رهگذر / يك سبوشان كرد دست كوزه‏گر
entries.english: As dust scattered on the thoroughfare: the hand of the Potter made them one jug

entries.farsi: كه اتحاد جسمهاى آب و طين / ‏هست ناقص جان نمى‏ماند بدين
entries.english: For the oneness of bodies of water and clay is imperfect: the soul is not like this.

entries.farsi: گر نظاير گويم اينجا در مثال / ‏فهم را ترسم كه آرد اختالل
entries.english: If I should here utter similarities in comparison, I fear it might disorder the understanding.

entries.farsi: هم سليمان هست اكنون ليك ما / از نشاط دور بينى در عما
entries.english: Even now there exists Solomon, but we are blinded by exulting in far-sightedness.

entries.farsi: دور بينى كور دارد مرد را / همچو خفته در سرا كور از سرا
entries.english: Far-sightedness keeps a man blind, just as one sleeping in a house is blind to the house.

entries.farsi: مولعيم اندر سخنهاى دقيق / ‏در گرهها باز كردن ما عشيق
entries.english: We are much addicted to subtle discussions; we are exceedingly fond of solving problems;

entries.farsi: تا گره بنديم و بگشاييم ما / در شكال و در جواب آيين فزا
entries.english: And to the end that we may tie knots and undo them, making many rules for the difficulty and for answering,

entries.farsi: همچو مرغى كاو گشايد بند دام / ‏گاه بندد تا شود در فن تمام
entries.english: Like a bird which should undo the fastenings of a snare, and tie at times, in order that it might become perfect in skill:

entries.farsi: او بود محروم از صحرا و مرج / ‏عمر او اندر گره كارى است خرج
entries.english: It is deprived of the open country and meadowland, its life is spent in dealing with knots;

entries.farsi: خود زبون او نگردد هيچ دام / ‏ليك پرش در شكست افتد مدام
entries.english: And even the snare is nowise subdued by it, but its wings are always getting broken.

entries.farsi: با گره كم كوش تا بال و پرت / ‏نگسلد يك يك از اين كر و فرت
entries.english: Do not struggle with knots, lest your wings and feathers be snapped asunder one by one through this vain display on your part.

entries.farsi: صد هزاران مرغ پرهاشان شكست / ‏و آن كمين گاه عوارض را نبست
entries.english: Myriads of birds have had their wings broken, and have not stopped that calamitous ambuscade.

entries.farsi: حال ايشان از نبى خوان اى حريص / ‏نقبوا فيها ببين هَلْ مِنْ محيص
entries.english: Read in the Qur’an their state, O covetous one: — They explored in them; mark, Was there any refuge?

entries.farsi: از نزاع ترك و رومى و عرب / ‏حل نشد اشكال انگور و عنب
entries.english: The difficulty over angur and inab was not solved by the contest between the Turk, the Greek, and the Arab.

entries.farsi: تا سليمان لسين معنوى / ‏در نيايد بر نخيزد اين دوى
entries.english: Until the spiritual Solomon; skilled in tongues, shall intervene, this duality will not disappear.

entries.farsi: جمله مرغان منازع بازوار / بشنويد اين طبل باز شهريار
entries.english: O all you wrangling birds, hearken, like the falcon, to this falcon-drum of the King.

entries.farsi: ز اختالف خويش سوى اتحاد / هين ز هر جانب روان گرديد شاد
entries.english: Hark, from every quarter set out with joy, from your diversity towards oneness.

entries.farsi: حيث ما كنتم فولوا وجهكم / ‏نحوه هذا الذى لم ينهكم
entries.english: Wherever you are, turn your faces towards it: this is the thing which He has not forbidden unto you.

entries.farsi: كور مرغانيم و بس ناساختيم / ‏كان سليمان را دمى نشناختيم
entries.english: Blind birds are we and very inept, in that we have not once recognised that Solomon.

entries.farsi: همچو جغدان دشمن بازان شديم / ‏الجرم وامانده‏ى ويران شديم
entries.english: Like the owls, we have become hostile to the falcons: consequently we are left behind in the place of ruin.

entries.farsi: مى‏كنيم از غايت جهل و عما / قصد آزار عزيزان خدا
entries.english: Because. of extreme ignorance and blindness we are seeking to hurt those honoured of God.

entries.farsi: جمع مرغان كز سليمان روشنند / پر و بال بى‏گنه كى بر كنند
entries.english: How should the flock of birds which are enlightened by Solomon tear out the wings and plumes of the innocent?

entries.farsi: بلكه سوى عاجزان چينه كشند / بى‏خالف و كينه آن مرغان خوشند
entries.english: Nay, they would bring grain to the helpless ones: gentle are those birds and without pugnacity or hatred.

entries.farsi: هدهد ايشان پى تقديس را / مى‏گشايد راه صد بلقيس را
entries.english: Their hoopoe, for the sake of glorifying, unfolds the way to a hundred like Bilqis.

entries.farsi: زاغ ايشان گر به صورت زاغ بود / باز همت آمد و ما زاغ بود
entries.english: Their crow, if it was a crow in outward aspect, was a falcon in aspiration and turned not aside.

entries.farsi: لكلك ايشان كه لك لك مى‏زند / آتش توحيد در شك مى‏زند
entries.english: Their stork, which cries lak lak, casts upon doubt the fire of the profession of Unity;

entries.farsi: و آن كبوترشان ز بازان نشكهد / باز سر پيش كبوترشان نهد
entries.english: And their dove is not scared by hawks: the hawk lays his head before their dove.

entries.farsi: بلبل ايشان كه حالت آرد او / در درون خويش گلشن دارد او
entries.english: Their nightingale, which throws you into ecstasy, has the rose-garden in its heart.

entries.farsi: طوطى ايشان ز قند آزاد بود / كز درون قند ابد رويش نمود
entries.english: Their parrot was independent of sugar, for the sugar of everlastingness showed its face to it from within.

entries.farsi: پاى طاوسان ايشان در نظر / بهتر از طاوس پران دگر
entries.english: The feet of their peacocks are fairer to see than others in peacock’s plumage.

entries.farsi: منطق الطيران خاقانى صداست / ‏منطق الطير سليمانى كجاست
entries.english: The speeches of princely birds’ are an echo: where is the speech of the birds of Solomon?

entries.farsi: تو چه دانى بانگ مرغان را همى / ‏چون نديده‏ستى سليمان را دمى
entries.english: How will you know the cries of the birds, when you have never seen Solomon for a single moment?

entries.farsi: هر يك آهنگش ز كرسى تاثرى است / ‏وز ثرى تا عرش در كر و فرى است
entries.english: The wings of that bird whose note thrills are beyond East and West.

entries.farsi: پر آن مرغى كه بانگش مطرب است / ‏از برون مشرق است و مغرب است
entries.english: Its every course is from the Footstool of God to the earth, and from the earth to the Throne of God it moves in glory and majesty.

entries.farsi: مرغ كاو بى‏اين سليمان مى‏رود / عاشق ظلمت چو خفاشى بود
entries.english: The bird that goes without this Solomon is in love with darkness, like a bat

entries.farsi: با سليمان خو كن اى خفاش رد / تا كه در ظلمت نمانى تا ابد
entries.english: Make yourself familiar with Solomon, O reprobate bat, in order that you may not remain in darkness for ever.

entries.farsi: يك گزى ره كه بدان سو مى‏روى / ‏همچو گز قطب مساحت مى‏شوى
entries.english: When you go one yard length in that direction, like the yard you will become the standard of measurement;

entries.farsi: و انكه لنگ و لوك آن سو مى‏جهى / ‏از همه لنگى و لوكى مى‏رهى
entries.english: And your hopping lamely and limply in that direction, you will be freed from all lameness and limpness.

sections.title_fa: قصه‏ى بط بچگان كه مرغ خانگى پروردشان
sections.title_en: The story of the ducklings which were fostered by a domestic fowl

entries.farsi: تخم بطى گر چه مرغ خانه‏ات / ‏كرد زير پر چو دايه تربيت
entries.english: You are the offspring of a duck, though a domestic fowl has nursed you beneath her wing.

entries.farsi: مادر تو بط آن دريا بده‏ست / ‏دايه‏ات خاكى بد و خشكى پرست
entries.english: Your mother was the duck of that Sea; your nurse was of the earth and devoted to the dry land.

entries.farsi: ميل دريا كه دل تو اندر است / ‏آن طبيعت جانت را از مادر است
entries.english: The desire which is in your heart for the Sea— your soul has that nature from your mother.

entries.farsi: ميل خشكى مر ترا زين دايه است / ‏دايه را بگذار كاو بد رايه است
entries.english: The desire you have for the dry land is from this nurse. Leave the nurse, for she is an evil counsellor.

entries.farsi: دايه را بگذار در خشك و بران / ‏اندر آن در بحر معنى چون بطان
entries.english: Leave the nurse on the dry land, and press on: come into the Sea of spiritual reality, like the ducks.

entries.farsi: گر ترا مادر بترساند ز آب / ‏تو مترس و سوى دريا ران شتاب
entries.english: If your mother should bid you be afraid of the water, fear not, but push speedily into the Sea

entries.farsi: تو بطى بر خشك و بر تر زنده‏اى / ‏نى چو مرغ خانه خانه كنده‏اى
entries.english: You area duck: you are one that lives on dry and wet; you are not one like the domestic fowl, whose house is stinking.

entries.farsi: تو ز كَرَّمْنا بَنِي آدَمَ شهى / ‏هم به خشكى هم به دريا پا نهى
entries.english: You are a king in virtue of, We have ennobled the sons of Adam: you set foot both on the dry land and on the Sea.

entries.farsi: كه حملناهم على البحرى به جان / ‏از حملناهم على البر پيش ران
entries.english: For in spirit you are, We have conveyed them on the Sea: push forward from, We have conveyed them on the land.

entries.farsi: مر ماليك را سوى بر راه نيست / ‏جنس حيوان هم ز بحر آگاه نيست
entries.english: The angels have no access to the land; the animal kind, again, are ignorant of the Sea.

entries.farsi: تو به تن حيوان به جانى از ملك / ‏تا روى هم بر زمين هم بر فلك
entries.english: You in body are an animal, and in spirit you are of the angels, so that you may walk on the earth and also in the sky;

entries.farsi: تا به ظاهر مثلكم باشد بشر / با دل يوحي إليه ديده‏ور
entries.english: So that the seer with heart divinely inspired may be, in appearance, a man like yourselves.

entries.farsi: قالب خاكى فتاده بر زمين / ‏روح آن گردان بر اين چرخ برين
entries.english: His body of dust, fallen upon the earth; his spirit circling beyond highest sphere.

entries.farsi: ما همه مرغابيانيم اى غالم / ‏بحر مى‏داند زبان ما تمام
entries.english: We all are water-birds, O lad: the Sea fully knows our language.

entries.farsi: پس سليمان بحر آمد ما چو طير / در سليمان تا ابد داريم سير
entries.english: Therefore the Sea is Solomon, and we are as the birds; in Solomon we move unto everlasting.

entries.farsi: با سليمان پاى در دريا بنه / ‏تا چو داود آب سازد صد زره
entries.english: With Solomon set your foot in the Sea, that the water, David-like, may make a hundred rings of mail.

entries.farsi: آن سليمان پيش جمله حاضر است / ‏ليك غيرت چشم بند و ساحر است
entries.english: That Solomon is present to all, but jealousy binds eyes and enchants,

entries.farsi: تا ز جهل و خوابناكى و فضول / ‏او به پيش ما و ما از وى ملول
entries.english: So that from folly and drowsiness and vanity— He is beside us, and we are sick of Him.

entries.farsi: تشنه را درد سر آرد بانگ رعد / چون نداند كاو كشاند ابر سعد
entries.english: The noise of thunder gives the thirsty man headache, when he does not know that it brings on the rain-clouds of felicity.

entries.farsi: چشم او مانده است در جوى روان / ‏بى‏خبر از ذوق آب آسمان
entries.english: His eye remains upon the running stream, unaware of the delicious taste of the Water of Heaven.

entries.farsi: مركب همت سوى اسباب راند / از مسبب الجرم محجوب ماند
entries.english: He has urged the steed of attention towards causes: consequently he remains debarred from the Causer.

entries.farsi: آن كه بيند او مسبب را عيان / ‏كى نهد دل بر سببهاى جهان
entries.english: One that sees the Causer plainly— how should he set his mind upon the causes in the world?

sections.title_fa: حيران شدن حاجيان در كرامات آن زاهد كه در باديه تنهاش يافتند
sections.title_en: How the pilgrims were amazed at the miracles of the ascetic whom they found alone in the desert.

entries.farsi: حاجيان آن جا رسيدند از بالد / ديده‏شان بر زاهد خشك اوفتاد
entries.english: Amidst the desert lived an ascetic, absorbed in devotion like the people of Abbadan.

entries.farsi: زاهدى بد در ميان باديه / ‏در عبادت غرق چون عباديه
entries.english: The pilgrims from countries arrived there: their eyes fell upon the parched ascetic.

entries.farsi: جاى زاهد خشك بود او تر مزاج / ‏از سموم باديه بودش عالج
entries.english: The dwelling-place of the ascetic was dry, he was moist in temperament: in the dry wind of the desert he had a remedy.

entries.farsi: حاجيان حيران شدند از وحدتش / ‏و آن سالمت در ميان آفتش
entries.english: The pilgrims were amazed at his solitude and his welfare in the midst of ruin.

entries.farsi: در نماز استاده بد بر روى ريگ / ‏ريگ كز تفش بجوشد آب ديگ
entries.english: He stood on the sand, in the ritual prayer— sand so hot, the heat of it would boil the water in a pot.

entries.farsi: گفتيى سر مست در سبزه و گل است / ‏يا سواره بر براق و دلدل است
entries.english: You would have said he was enraptured amongst herbs and flowers, or mounted on Buraq or Duldul;

entries.farsi: يا كه پايش بر حرير و حله‏هاست / ‏يا سموم او را به از باد صباست
entries.english: Or that his feet were on silk and broidered cloths; or that to him the sandstorm was more pleasant than the zephyr.

entries.farsi: پس بماندند آن جماعت با نياز / تا شود درويش فارغ از نماز
entries.english: They stood waiting; he remained standing in prayer, in long meditation.

entries.farsi: چون ز استغراق باز آمد فقير / ز آن جماعت زنده‏اى روشن ضمير
entries.english: When the dervish came back from absorption, one of that company, a man alive and of enlightened mind,

entries.farsi: ديد كابش مى‏چكيد از دست و رو / جامه‏اش تر بود از آثار وضو
entries.english: Observed that water was trickling from his hands and face, his garment was wet with the traces of ablution;

entries.farsi: پس بپرسيدش كه آبت از كجاست / ‏دست را برداشت كز سوى شماست
entries.english: So he asked him, “From where do you have water?” He lifted his hand, indicating it came from heaven.

entries.farsi: گفت هر گاهى كه خواهى مى‏رسد / بى‏ز جاه و بى‏ز حبل من مسد
entries.english: He said, “Does it come whenever you will, without well and without rope of palm-fibre?

entries.farsi: مشكل ما حل كن اى سلطان دين / ‏تا ببخشد حال تو ما را يقين
entries.english: Solve our difficulty, O Sultan of the Religion, in order that your experience may give us certain faith.

entries.farsi: وانما سرى ز اسرارت به ما / تا ببريم از ميان زنارها
entries.english: Reveal to us one of your mysteries that we may cut from our waists the cords.”

entries.farsi: چشم را بگشود سوى آسمان / ‏كه اجابت كن دعاى حاجيان
entries.english: He turned his eyes to heaven, saying, “Answer the prayer of the pilgrims!

entries.farsi: رزق جويى را ز بالا خو گرم / ‏تو ز بالا بر گشودستى درم
entries.english: I am accustomed to seeking daily bread from above: You have opened to me the door from above,

entries.farsi: اى نموده تو مكان از المكان / ‏فِي السَّماءِ رِزْقُكُمْ كرده عيان
entries.english: O You who from non-spatiality has brought space into view, and has made manifest in heaven is your daily bread.”

entries.farsi: در ميان اين مناجات ابر خوش / ‏زود پيدا شد چو پيل آب كش
entries.english: In the midst of this orison a fair cloud suddenly appeared, like a water-bearing elephant,

entries.farsi: همچو آب از مشك باريدن گرفت / ‏در گو و در غارها مسكن گرفت
entries.english: And began to pour down rain, like water from a water-skin: the rain-water settled in the ditch and in the hollows.

entries.farsi: ابر مى‏باريد چون مشك اشكها / حاجيان جمله گشاده مشكها
entries.english: The cloud kept raining tears, like a water-skin, and the pilgrims all opened their water-skins.

entries.farsi: يك جماعت ز آن عجايب كارها / مى‏بريدند از ميان زنارها
entries.english: One party, in consequence of those marvellous happenings, was cutting the cords from their waists.

entries.farsi: قوم ديگر را يقين در ازدياد / زين عجب و اللهَّ أعلم بالرشاد
entries.english: The certainty of another group was on the increase because of this miracle—and God knows best how to guide aright.

entries.farsi: قوم ديگر ناپذيرا ترش و خام / ‏ناقصان سرمدى تم الكالم
entries.english: Another group unreceptive, sour and unripe, eternally imperfect ones. Ends the discourse.

sections.title_fa: پایان دفتر دوم
